text.text: When you take the Attack action, you can make one attack roll with a weapon or an

You can either equip or unequip one weapon when you make an attack as part of this action. You do so either before or after the attack. If you equip a weapon before an attack, you don't need to use it for that attack. Equipping a weapon includes drawing it from a sheath or picking it up. Unequipping a weapon includes sheathing, stowing, or dropping it.

If you move on your turn and have a feature, such as Extra Attack, that gives you more than one attack as part of the Attack action, you can use some or all of that movement to move between those attacks.

docs.title: Attack Action
docs.excerpt: The most common action taken in combat. When you take the Attack action, you make one or more melee or ranged attacks.

text.text: The most common
taken in combat is the Attack action. When you take the Attack action, you make one melee or ranged attack.
Making an Attack
Choose a target
within your attack's range
Determine modifiers
(advantage/disadvantage, cover, special abilities)
Resolve the attack
by rolling a d20 and adding modifiers
Roll damage
if the attack hits
Multiple Attacks
Certain features, such as the Extra Attack feature of fighters and paladins, allow you to make more than one attack with this action. Each attack is a separate attack roll.
Level 5
characters of certain classes can attack twice
Level 11
fighters can attack three times
Level 20
fighters can attack four times
Attack Types
Used when attacking a target within your reach (usually 5 feet). Uses Strength modifier for the attack and damage rolls (unless you're using a finesse weapon).
Used when attacking a target at a distance with a ranged weapon or thrown weapon. Uses Dexterity modifier for the attack and damage rolls (unless it's a thrown weapon, which can use Strength).
You can make an
instead of using a weapon. An unarmed strike counts as a melee weapon attack.
Two-Weapon Fighting
When you take the Attack action and attack with a light melee weapon in one hand, you can use a
to attack with a different light melee weapon in your other hand.

docs.title: Attune during a Short Rest
docs.excerpt: No excerpt available.

text.text: Attuning to an item requires you to spend a Short Rest focused on only that item while being in physical contact with it (this can't be the same Short Rest used to learn the item's properties). This focus can take the form of weapon practice (for a Weapon), meditation (for a Wand), or some other appropriate activity. If the Short Rest is interrupted, the Attunement attempt fails. Otherwise, at the end of the Short Rest, you're attuned to the magic item and can access its full magical capabilities.

docs.title: Bloodied
docs.excerpt: A creature is bloodied when its current hit points are at or below half its hit point maximum and it still has at least 1 hit point.

text.text: A creature is bloodied when its current hit points are at or below half its hit point maximum and it still has at least 1 hit point remaining.
Rules Use
Bloodied is a current health-state threshold, not a permanent trait
A creature becomes bloodied as soon as its current hit points fall to half its maximum or lower
A creature stops being bloodied if healing raises its current hit points above that threshold
Bloodied does not mean defeated, unconscious, or dead
A creature at 0 hit points has crossed into a different crisis state
Rules that care about bloodied creatures are looking for a wounded but still active target unless they say otherwise

title.text: Temporary Hit Points Do Not Change The Threshold

text.text: The bloodied threshold is based on normal hit point maximum and current hit points
Temporary hit points can absorb damage before normal hit points are lost
Temporary hit points do not raise the maximum and do not by themselves remove a creature from being bloodied
Resolution Meaning

Bloodied is a live world-state signal that a creature has been worn down past its midpoint but is still in play. Other rules can use that state as a trigger, requirement, or limiter for effects that care about visible injury or narrowing reserves.

docs.title: Bonus Action
docs.excerpt: A Bonus Action is a special action that you can take on the same turn that you take an action. You can't take more than one Bonus Action on a turn, an...

text.text: A Bonus Action is a special action that you can take on the same turn that you take an action. You can't take more than one Bonus Action on a turn, and you have a Bonus Action to take only if a rule explicitly says so.

docs.title: Bonus Actions
docs.excerpt: An additional action on your turn, which can only be taken when a special ability, spell, or other feature says you can.

text.text: Various class features, spells, and other abilities let you take an additional action on your turn called a Bonus Action. The Cunning Action feature, for example, allows a Rogue to take a Bonus Action. You can take a Bonus Action only when a special ability, a spell, or another feature of the game states that you can do something as a Bonus Action. You otherwise don't have a Bonus Action to take.
You can take only one Bonus Action on your turn, so you must choose which Bonus Action to use if you have more than one available.

You choose when to take a Bonus Action during your turn unless the Bonus Action's timing is specified. Anything that deprives you of your ability to take actions also prevents you from taking a Bonus Action.

docs.excerpt: Objects can be harmed by attacks and by some spells, using the rules below. If an object is exceedingly fragile, the DM may allow a creature to break ...

text.text: Objects can be harmed by attacks and by some spells, using the rules below. If an object is exceedingly fragile, the DM may allow a creature to break it automatically with the
or
action.
The Object
table suggests ACs for various substances.
Object Armor Class
An object is destroyed when it has 0 Hit Points. The Object Hit Points table suggests
for fragile and resilient objects that are Large or smaller. To track Hit Points for a Huge or Gargantuan object, divide it into Large or smaller sections, and track each section's
separately. The DM determines whether destroying part of an object causes the whole thing to collapse.
Object Hit Points
Objects have
to Poison and Psychic damage. The DM might decide that some
are more or less effective against an object. For example, Bludgeoning damage works well for smashing things but not for cutting. Paper or cloth objects might have
to Fire damage.
Big objects, such as castle walls, often have extra resilience represented by a damage threshold.
An object lacks ability scores unless a rule assigns scores to the object. Without ability scores, an object can't make ability checks, and it fails all saving throws.

docs.title: Brown Mold
docs.excerpt: Brown mold resembles a furry, light-brown carpet. This fungus feeds on warmth, drawing heat from anything around itself. One patch of brown mold cover...

text.text: Brown mold resembles a furry, light-brown carpet. This fungus feeds on warmth, drawing heat from anything around itself. One patch of brown mold covers a 10-foot square, and the temperature within 30 feet of it is always frigid.

When a creature enters a space within 5 feet of the mold for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there, the creature makes a DC 12 Constitution saving throw, taking 22 (4d10) Cold damage on a failed save or half as much damage on a successful one.
Brown mold has

to Fire damage, and any source of fire brought within 5 feet of it causes the mold to instantly expand across a surface and toward the fire, creating a new patch that covers a 10-foot square. A patch of brown mold exposed to an effect that deals any amount of Cold damage is destroyed instantly.

docs.title: Charisma
docs.excerpt: Measures force of personality, persuasiveness, and leadership ability. Charisma is important for bards, paladins, sorcerers, and warlocks.

text.text: Charisma measures your ability to interact effectively with others. It includes such factors as confidence and eloquence, and it can represent a charming or commanding personality.
Charisma Checks
A Charisma check might arise when you try to influence or entertain others, when you try to make an impression or tell a convincing lie, or when you are navigating a tricky social situation.
Your Charisma (Deception) check determines your ability to:
Hide the truth convincingly
Tell lies
Create false identities
Use disguises
Make ambiguous statements
Your Charisma (Intimidation) check determines your ability to:
Influence someone through overt threats
Show hostile actions
Display physical violence
Extract information through threats
Your Charisma (Performance) check determines your ability to:
Delight an audience with music, dance, acting, storytelling, or other entertainment
Play an instrument
Tell jokes
Recite poetry
Your Charisma (Persuasion) check determines your ability to:
Influence someone with tact, social graces, or good nature
Foster friendships
Make cordial requests
Exhibit proper etiquette
Act in good faith
Spellcasting Ability
Charisma is the spellcasting ability for:
Bards
: Magic from words of power and music
Paladins
: Divine magic from sacred oaths
Sorcerers
: Innate magic from magical bloodlines
Warlocks
: Magic granted by otherworldly patrons
Charisma Saving Throws
Charisma saving throws are less common but represent your ability to resist effects that would:
Banish
you to another plane
Possess
or control your body
Exile
you from reality
Destroy your sense of self
Banishment
Dispel Evil and Good
Divine Word
Planar Binding

docs.title: Coins
docs.excerpt: Rules on currency denominations and selling equipment.

text.text: Characters often find coins on their adventures and can spend those coins in shops, inns, and other businesses. Coins come in different denominations based on the relative worth of their material. The Coin Values table lists coins and how much they're worth relative to the Gold Piece, which is the game's main coin. For example, 100 Copper Pieces are worth 1 Gold Piece.
A coin weighs about a third of an ounce, so fifty coins weigh a pound.

Equipment fetches half its cost when sold. In contrast, trade goods and valuables-like gems and art objects-retain their full value in the marketplace. The Magic Items by Rarity section of Magic Items has prices for magic items.

docs.title: Concentration
docs.excerpt: Some spells require you to maintain concentration to keep their magic active. If you lose concentration, such a spell ends immediately.

text.text: Some spells require you to maintain concentration in order to keep their magic active. If you lose concentration, such a spell ends.
Concentration Rules

If a spell must be maintained with concentration, that fact appears in its Duration entry, and the spell specifies how long you can concentrate on it. You can end concentration at any time (no action required).
You can't concentrate on two spells at once. If you cast another spell that requires concentration, the first spell ends.
Normal activity, such as moving and attacking, doesn't interfere with concentration. The following factors can break concentration:
Breaking Concentration
Whenever you take damage while concentrating on a spell, you must make a
saving throw to maintain your concentration. The DC equals:
, or
Half the damage
you take (rounded down)
Whichever number is
higher
Multiple Sources
: If you take damage from multiple sources, such as an arrow and a dragon's breath, you make a separate saving throw for each source of damage.
You lose concentration on a spell if you are incapacitated or if you die.

The game might also decide that certain environmental phenomena, such as a wave crashing over you while you're on a storm-tossed ship, require you to succeed on a DC 10 Constitution saving throw to maintain concentration on a spell.
Common Concentration Spells
Many powerful spells require concentration:
Bless
Hunter's Mark
Hex
Haste
Invisibility
Detect Magic
Fly
Hypnotic Pattern
Polymorph
Greater Invisibility

docs.title: Condition
docs.excerpt: A condition is a temporary game state. The definition of a condition says how it affects its recipient, and various rules define how to end a conditio...

text.text: A condition is a temporary game state. The definition of a condition says how it affects its recipient, and various rules define how to end a condition. This glossary defines these conditions:
A condition doesn't stack with itself; a recipient either has a condition or doesn't. The
condition is an exception to that rule.

docs.title: Conditions (Overview)
docs.excerpt: Many effects impose a condition, a temporary state that alters the recipient's capabilities. The specific conditions, their durations, and stacking rules are detailed in sub-entries.

text.text: Many effects in the game impose a condition, a temporary state that alters the recipient's capabilities. A condition's definition specifies what happens to its recipient while affected by it, and some conditions apply other conditions.
The specific conditions, their durations, and how they interact are detailed in the sub-entries in the glossary sidebar.

docs.title: Conditions Don't Stack
docs.excerpt: If multiple effects impose the same condition, the effects don't get worse. The Exhaustion condition is an exception.

text.text: If multiple effects impose the same condition on you, each instance of the condition has its own duration, but the condition's effects don't get worse. Either you have a condition or you don't.
The
condition is an exception; its effects get worse if you have the condition and receive it again.

docs.title: Cone
docs.excerpt: A cone is a three-dimensional area of effect that projects outward from a point of origin, with its width expanding proportionally to its distance.

text.text: A cone is a three-dimensional spatial volume that projects from a point of origin and expands proportionally as it travels. It is commonly used for directional projections, such as sprays, breath weapons, and forward bursts.
Geometry and Expansion
Point of Origin
: The effect begins at a designated starting point, which serves as the apex of the cone.
Directional Path
: The cone extends along a straight line in a direction chosen by its creator.
Proportional Scaling

: The width of the cone at any point along its length is exactly equal to that point's distance from the point of origin. For example, at a distance of 15 feet from the origin, the cone is 15 feet wide; at 30 feet from the origin, it is 30 feet wide.
Length Boundary
: The effect specifying the cone defines its maximum length, which caps the expansion of the shape.
Origin Interaction

A cone's point of origin is situated at the very tip of the apex. This point is not included in the area of effect unless the creator decides otherwise, allowing a caster or device to unleash the projection without automatically exposing their own space to the effect.
Spatial Applications

Because the cone expands from a narrow point into a wide sweep, it is ideal for sweeping wide swathes of space directly ahead of the origin. It is highly directional, leaving the space behind and to the immediate sides of the point of origin completely unaffected.

docs.title: Cone [Area of Effect]
docs.excerpt: A Cone is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a point of origin in a direction its creator chooses. A Cone's width at any point alon...

text.text: A Cone is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a point of origin in a direction its creator chooses. A Cone's width at any point along its length is equal to that point's distance from the point of origin. For example, a Cone is 15 feet wide at a point along its length that is 15 feet from the point of origin. The effect that creates a Cone specifies its maximum length.
A Cone's point of origin isn't included in the area of effect unless its creator decides otherwise.

docs.title: Constitution
docs.excerpt: Measures health, stamina, and vital force. Constitution determines your hit points and affects concentration on spells.

text.text: Constitution measures health, stamina, and vital force.
Constitution Checks

Constitution checks are uncommon, and no skills apply to Constitution checks, because the endurance this ability represents is largely passive rather than involving a specific effort on the part of a character or monster.
Holding your breath
Marching or laboring
for hours without rest
Going without sleep
Surviving without food or water
Quaffing an entire stein of ale
in one go
Hit Points
Your Constitution modifier contributes to your
. Typically, you add your Constitution modifier to each Hit Die you roll for your hit points.
If your Constitution modifier changes, your hit point maximum changes as well, as though you had the new modifier from 1st level.
Concentration
When you take damage while
on a spell, you must make a Constitution saving throw to maintain your concentration. The DC equals 10 or half the damage you take, whichever number is higher.
Constitution Saving Throws
Constitution saving throws are common and represent your ability to resist:
Poison
and disease
Exhaustion
from forced marches
Concentration
when taking damage while casting or maintaining a spell
Environmental hazards
like extreme heat or cold
Death
(in the form of death saving throws when at 0 HP)
Poison Spray
Cloudkill
Contagion
Blight

), and Total Cover (can't be targeted directly). If behind more than one degree of cover, a target benefits only from the most protective degree.

text.text: A ship larger than a Keelboat or Rowboat needs a crew of skilled hirelings (see the Services section later in this chapter) to function. The minimum number of skilled hirelings needed to crew a ship depends on the type of ship, as shown in the Airborne and Waterborne Vehicles table.

docs.title: Critical Hit
docs.excerpt: If you roll a 20 on the d20 for an attack roll, you score a Critical Hit, and the attack hits regardless of any modifiers or the target's AC. A Critic...

text.text: If you roll a 20 on the d20 for an attack roll, you score a Critical Hit, and the attack hits regardless of any modifiers or the target's AC. A Critical Hit lets you roll extra dice for the attack's damage against the target. Roll all of the attack's damage dice twice and add them together. Then add any relevant modifiers.

docs.title: Cube
docs.excerpt: A cube is a three-dimensional area of effect defined by a symmetric volume of equal length, width, and height, originating from a point along one of its faces.

text.text: A cube is a three-dimensional spatial volume defined by equal measurements of length, width, and height. It projects outward from a point of origin positioned on one of its boundary faces, making it a versatile tool for blocking passages or saturating structured rooms.
Geometry and Structure
Equal Dimensions
: The effect that creates a cube defines its size, which represents the length of each of its six faces. A 20-foot cube, for example, is 20 feet long, 20 feet wide, and 20 feet high.
Straight Lines
: The volume expands in straight paths from the point of origin, conforming perfectly to orthogonal spaces.
Origin and Placement
Face Placement

: Unlike shapes that expand radially from a central point, a cube's point of origin can be located anywhere along one of its flat faces. This allows creators to offset the volume relative to the point of origin.
Origin Inclusion

: The point of origin is not included in the cube's area of effect unless the creator decides otherwise. This allows Casters or triggering devices to align a face of the cube immediately adjacent to themselves without being engulfed by the volume.
Tactical Advantages

Because the point of origin lies on a face of the cube rather than its center, the shape can be projected entirely forward, upward, or downward from its source. It is highly effective for sealing corridors, filling rectangular chambers, or engulfing groups of targets without affecting the initiator.

docs.title: Cube [Area of Effect]
docs.excerpt: A Cube is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a point of origin located anywhere on a face of the Cube. The effect that creates a Cu...

text.text: A Cube is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a point of origin located anywhere on a face of the Cube. The effect that creates a Cube specifies its size, which is the length of each side. A Cube's point of origin isn't included in the area of effect unless its creator decides otherwise.

docs.title: Curse
docs.excerpt: A Curse is a harmful magical effect bound to a creature, object, or place. It persists for the duration stated by its source or until magic or another rule ends it.

text.text: A curse is a harmful effect attached to a creature, object, place, or ongoing state. Its exact burden is defined by the effect that created it, and that burden remains in force for the curse's stated duration or until something ends it.
Rules Use

A curse can weaken, restrict, compel, transform, bind, or otherwise afflict its subject. Some magic lifts a curse outright, while other effects continue until a specific condition, ritual, or stronger magic breaks them.
Resolution Meaning
A curse is not just a moment of harm. It is an attached adverse state that remains live in the world until its ending rule is satisfied.

docs.title: Curses
docs.excerpt: Some game effects curse a creature or an object. The effect that confers a curse defines what the curse does. Curses can be removed by the Remove Curs...

text.text: Some game effects curse a creature or an object. The effect that confers a curse defines what the curse does. Curses can be removed by the Remove Curse and Greater Restoration spells or other magic that explicitly ends curses.

docs.title: Cylinder
docs.excerpt: A cylinder is a three-dimensional area of effect defined by a circular footprint of a specific radius and a vertical column of a designated height.

text.text: A cylinder is a three-dimensional spatial volume defined by a circular base and a vertical column of height. It is commonly used for weather-like column effects, pillars of fire, celestial beams, and vertical spatial barriers.
Geometry and Profile
Circular Base
: The effect specifying a cylinder defines a radius, which determines the size of its circular footprint.
Vertical Column
: The effect also specifies a height, defining how far the vertical column extends upward or downward from its circular base.
Radial Expansion
: The volume extends outward in straight lines from a central vertical axis to the boundary of the circular radius.
Origin and Inclusion
Center Alignment
: The point of origin for a cylinder is located at the exact center of either the circular top or circular bottom of the column.
Origin Included

: Unlike cones and cubes, a cylinder's point of origin is always included within the area of effect itself. This means any creature or object situated exactly at the origin coordinates is automatically subjected to the cylinder's effects.
Tactical Applications

Cylinders are excellent for targeting vertical zones, enabling effects to reach flying creatures high above the ground or strike targets on multiple vertical levels (such as scaffolding, balconies, or pits). Because the origin rests at the base or top of the pillar, it allows casters to call down massive vertical columns at a distance without engulfing themselves.

docs.title: Cylinder [Area of Effect]
docs.excerpt: A Cylinder is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a point of origin located at the center of the circular top or bottom of the Cylin...

text.text: A Cylinder is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a point of origin located at the center of the circular top or bottom of the Cylinder. The effect that creates a Cylinder specifies the radius of the Cylinder's base and the Cylinder's height.
A Cylinder's point of origin is included in the area of effect.

docs.title: Dash [Action]
docs.excerpt: The Dash action spends a creature's action to add extra movement equal to its current speed for the turn.

text.text: The Dash action converts a creature's
into more movement for the current turn. When a creature Dashes, it gains extra movement equal to its current
Core Effect
Dash adds movement for the current turn instead of changing the creature's base speed
the extra movement is measured from the speed the creature currently has after other active modifiers are applied
the added movement can be spent in the same turn only; unused movement does not carry forward
Movement Modes
Dash supports whatever movement modes the creature can currently use. If the creature is moving across the ground,
, or
, the extra movement can be spent through that mode as long as the world state still allows it.
Limits
Dash does not remove terrain costs, movement penalties, or speed reductions
Dash does not prevent hostile reactions, including movement-based triggers
Dash does not let a creature move through blocked space or ignore other restrictions on movement

docs.title: Dexterity
docs.excerpt: Measures agility, reflexes, and balance. Dexterity is important for ranged combat, initiative, armor class, and avoiding area effects.

text.text: Dexterity measures agility, reflexes, and balance.
Dexterity Checks
A Dexterity check can model any attempt to move nimbly, quickly, or quietly, or to keep from falling on tricky footing.
Your Dexterity (Acrobatics) check covers your attempt to stay on your feet in a tricky situation, such as:
Running across a sheet of ice
Balancing on a tightrope
Staying upright on a rocking ship's deck
Performing acrobatic stunts (dives, rolls, somersaults, flips)
Your Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) check determines your ability to:
Pick pockets
Plant something on someone else
Conceal an object on your person
Perform legerdemain or magic tricks
Your Dexterity (Stealth) check determines your ability to:
Hide from enemies
Sneak past guards
Slip away without being noticed
Move silently
Controlling a heavily laden cart
on a steep descent
Steering a chariot
around a tight turn
Picking a lock
Disabling a trap
Securely tying up a prisoner
Wriggling free
of bonds
Playing a stringed instrument
Crafting a small or detailed object
Attack Rolls and Damage
You add your Dexterity modifier to:
Attack rolls
when using ranged weapons
Damage rolls
when attacking with a ranged weapon
Attack and damage rolls
when using finesse weapons (you choose between Strength or Dexterity)
Armor Class
Depending on the armor you wear, you might add some or all of your Dexterity modifier to your Armor Class.
Initiative
At the beginning of every combat, you roll initiative by making a Dexterity check. Initiative determines the order of creatures' turns in combat.

docs.title: Difficult Terrain
docs.excerpt: If a space is Difficult Terrain, every foot of movement in that space costs 1 extra foot. For example, moving 5 feet through Difficult Terrain costs 1...

text.text: If a space is Difficult Terrain, every foot of movement in that space costs 1 extra foot. For example, moving 5 feet through Difficult Terrain costs 10 feet of movement. Difficult Terrain isn't cumulative; either a space is Difficult Terrain or it isn't.
A space is Difficult Terrain if the space contains any of the following or something similar:
A creature that isn't Tiny or your ally
Furniture that is sized for creatures of your size or larger
Heavy snow, ice, rubble, or undergrowth
Liquid that's between shin- and waist-deep
A narrow opening sized for a creature one size smaller than you
A slope of 20 degrees or more

to careful withdrawal. For the rest of the current turn, that creature's movement does not trigger

the creature still spends its own movement normally after taking the action

Movement Context

A creature can Disengage before leaving melee, repositioning through a threatened area, or retreating to safer ground. The action does not create extra movement, so the creature is still limited by its
and any current movement costs.

or triggered effect

Disengage only changes the specific trigger that would normally allow an opportunity attack

Core Effect
Dodge represents a creature devoting its action to evasive movement, guarded posture, and threat awareness
the action improves the creature's defense against direct attacks and effects that can be avoided with quick movement
the benefit lasts until the start of the creature's next turn unless another rule prevents the creature from using it effectively

Dodge protects the creature through the rest of the current round and into other creatures' turns, ending when that creature's next turn begins.

Dodge does not grant extra movement, cover, or resistance by itself

docs.title: Emanation
docs.excerpt: An emanation is a three-dimensional area of effect that extends outward from a creature or object in all directions, moving dynamically with its origin.

text.text: An emanation is a three-dimensional area of effect that projects outward in all directions from a creature or object. Unlike static zones, an emanation is dynamically anchored to its source, moving along with the origin as it navigates space.
Mechanics and Boundaries
Radial Expansion

: The effect specifying an emanation defines a radius or distance, determining how far the volume extends in straight lines outward in all directions from the boundary of the origin creature or object.
Dynamic Anchoring

: The emanation moves automatically with the creature or object that serves as its point of origin. If the source creature moves 10 feet, the entire volume of the emanation shifts 10 feet along with it.
Stationary Exceptions

: If the effect is instantaneous (lasting only a fraction of a second) or explicitly stated to be stationary, it resolves immediately at the origin's initial position and does not continue to track movement.
Origin and Interaction
Origin Exclusion

: The originating creature or object is not included within the emanation's area of effect unless its creator decides otherwise. This allows Casters or specialized items to project protective, offensive, or sensory fields immediately surrounding themselves without being subject to their own spatial effects.
Tactical Applications

Emanations are highly effective for personal defense, local auras, and mobile hazards. CAS elements like holy shrouds, localized gravity distortion fields, and static clouds that drift with the user are typically modeled as emanations. They ensure a consistent defensive or offensive perimeter is maintained around an active combatant or payload.

docs.title: Emanation [Area of Effect]
docs.excerpt: An Emanation is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a creature or an object in all directions. The effect that creates an Emanation ...

text.text: An Emanation is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a creature or an object in all directions. The effect that creates an Emanation specifies the distance it extends.
An Emanation moves with the creature or object that is its origin unless it is an instantaneous or a stationary effect.
An Emanation's origin (creature or object) isn't included in the area of effect unless its creator decides otherwise.

You can use Empowered Spell even if you've already used a different Metamagic option during the casting of the spell.

This chapter provides rules and prices for weapons, armor, and other kinds of equipment that characters might purchase or find. The game tells you if a shop has an item for sale and whether it's available at the listed price.

text.text: As they overcome challenges and complete adventures, characters earn Experience Points (XP) which are awarded by the Dungeon Master. When a character's XP total crosses certain thresholds, the character's level increases. The Dungeon Master's Guide provides guidance on awarding XP.

docs.title: Finding Hidden Objects
docs.excerpt: Rules for searching for hidden objects.

text.text: When your character searches for hidden things, such as a secret door or a trap, the game typically asks you to make a Wisdom (Perception) check, provided you describe the character searching in the hidden object's vicinity. On a success, you find the object, other important details, or both.
If you describe your character searching nowhere near a hidden object, a Wisdom (Perception) check won't reveal the object, no matter the check's total.

to Psychic damage. When it drops to 0 Hit Points, the fungus explodes as if a

At Higher Levels

The explosion of a fireball fungus causes other fireball fungi in the area of effect to explode as well. You can scale the hazard for higher levels by adding one additional fungus at levels 11–16 or three additional fungi at levels 17–20.

docs.title: Glossary Conventions
docs.excerpt: Explains how to read Aralia's glossary entries, tags, rule chips, and spell backlinks.

text.text: Aralia's glossary uses a few repeated patterns so rules, spells, and linked concepts can be explored without guessing what a label does.
Entry Types
Rules entries
define a game concept, rule, or mechanic.
Spell entries
show spell data from the spell JSON corpus.
Generated referenced-rule entries
are created when spell canonical data points at a rule that should be browseable in the glossary.
Links And Chips
Inline rule chips
appear inside spell text when a spell description references a known rule term.
Spell Tags
can also include rule chips such as
Area: Sphere
when the spell references a rule family that is useful as metadata.
Clicking a chip
navigates directly to that glossary rule entry.
Hovering a chip
shows a short glossary excerpt so the user can preview the rule before opening it.
Cross-References
See Also
links connect one glossary entry to related rules.
Referenced By Spells
lists which spells currently point at a rule.
When a rule is linked from spell canonical data, the rule entry can act as a hub back into the spell system.
Reading Generated Rule Entries

Some rule entries are generated from canonical spell references rather than hand-authored first. Those entries still exist as real glossary destinations, but they should be treated as enrichment surfaces that help connect spells to rule concepts.
Scope
This glossary is meant to help users move between mechanics, spell details, and related concepts quickly. It is a navigation and interpretation surface, not just a flat index of isolated entries.

docs.title: Green Slime
docs.excerpt: Acidic green slime devours flesh, organic material, and metal on contact. Bright green, wet, and sticky, it clings to walls, floors, and ceilings in p...

text.text: Acidic green slime devours flesh, organic material, and metal on contact. Bright green, wet, and sticky, it clings to walls, floors, and ceilings in patches.
One patch of green slime covers a 5-foot square. Green slime has

with a range of 30 feet, and it drops from walls and ceilings when it detects movement below itself. Beyond that, it can't move. A creature aware of the slime's presence can avoid being struck by it with a successful DC 10 Dexterity saving throw.

A creature that comes in contact with green slime takes 5 (1d10) Acid damage. The creature takes the damage again at the start of each of its turns until the slime is scraped off (requiring an action) or destroyed. Against wood or metal, green slime deals 11 (2d10) Acid damage each round, and any nonmagical wood or metal item used to scrape off the green slime is destroyed.
Direct sunlight or any amount of Cold, Fire, or Radiant damage destroys a patch of green slime.

docs.title: Half Cover
docs.excerpt: Half cover provides partial protection when an obstacle screens at least half of a target's body.

text.text: Half cover represents partial physical screening by an intervening object, creature, or obstacle. It offers a modest degree of protection against direct attacks and spatial effects.
Environmental Context

A target has half cover if an obstacle blocks at least half of its physical form from the source of an attack or effect. Examples include a low wall, a pile of rubble, a thick tree trunk, or another creature standing in the path of the effect.
Tactical Protection
provides a +2 bonus to
provides a +2 bonus to
saving throws
Targeting Dynamics
Half cover does not prevent a source from targeting a creature directly, but the physical obstruction makes finding a clear path or clean hit more difficult. It scales into
as the barrier becomes more prominent.

docs.title: Help [Action]
docs.excerpt: The Help action lets a creature spend its action to improve another creature's attempt or to assist with another immediate task in the current scene.

text.text: The Help action commits a creature's
to assisting another creature instead of acting directly on its own behalf.
Rules
Help improves another creature's immediate chance to succeed at a task, attack, or other resolution that can benefit from timely assistance
Help works by adding support, positioning, warning, distraction, or another cooperative contribution that matters to the current situation
the assistance must remain relevant when the aided creature resolves the supported action or check
Common Uses
aiding an
during a combat exchange
helping another creature notice, interpret, or manipulate something in the environment
contributing to a coordinated effort where timing or shared effort matters
Limits
Help does not replace the aided creature's own resolution. It changes the conditions around that resolution by making support available at the right time.

or behind cover Quarters Cover or Total Cover, and you must be out of any enemy's line of sight; if you can see a creature, you can discern whether it can see you.
On a successful check, you have the

You stop being hidden immediately after any of the following occurs: you make a sound louder than a whisper, an enemy finds you, you make an attack roll, or you cast a spell with a Verbal component.

docs.title: Hide [Action]
docs.excerpt: A creature uses the Hide action to become harder to detect. Hiding depends on line of sight, cover, obscuration, noise, and other conditions that affect whether observers can locate the creature reliably.

text.text: The Hide action changes how easily a creature can be detected. A hiding creature is not automatically absent from the scene; instead, its position, outline, or presence becomes uncertain to observers that do not have a clear way to perceive it.
Rules
A creature uses its
to attempt to hide
Hiding depends on whether observers have a clear way to see or otherwise perceive the creature
Cover, darkness, fog, foliage, and other forms of obscuration can create space for a hiding attempt
A creature that is openly visible and clearly exposed usually does not remain hidden for long
A creature can remain unseen without being hidden, and a creature can stop being hidden when noise, movement, or exposure gives away its location
Detection And Position

A hidden creature forces other creatures to rely on incomplete information about where it is. Observers may still know the general area, hear movement, or infer a likely position, but the creature is no longer being tracked with the same certainty as a plainly visible target.
Bridge To Hiding
Aralia keeps a separate
glossary term as a bridge for the broader concept. Use this entry when the rule question is specifically about spending an action to become harder to detect.
Related Visibility States
limit sight and often create the conditions needed to hide
spaces make visual tracking unreliable
conceals a creature from ordinary sight, but invisibility and hiding are not identical states
Ending A Hidden State

A hidden state commonly ends when the creature becomes clearly exposed, makes its location obvious, or otherwise gives observers enough information to track it again. Attacking, leaving concealment, or moving through an exposed position often ends the hidden state even if the creature is still in the same encounter.

docs.title: Hiding
docs.excerpt: Rules for hiding from enemies.

text.text: Adventurers and monsters often hide, whether to spy on one another, sneak past a guardian, or set an ambush. The Dungeon Master decides when circumstances are appropriate for hiding. When you try to hide, you take the Hide action.

docs.title: High Altitude
docs.excerpt: Traveling at altitudes of 10,000 feet or higher above sea level is taxing for most creatures because of the reduced amount of oxygen in the air. Each ...

text.text: Traveling at altitudes of 10,000 feet or higher above sea level is taxing for most creatures because of the reduced amount of oxygen in the air. Each hour such a creature spends traveling at high altitude counts as 2 hours for the purpose of determining how long that creature can travel (see "Travel Pace" in chapter 2).

Creatures can become acclimated to a high altitude by spending 30 days or more at this elevation. Creatures can't become acclimated to elevations above 20,000 feet unless they are native to such environments.

docs.title: High Jump
docs.excerpt: When you make a High Jump, you leap into the air a number of feet equal to 3 plus your Strength modifier (minimum of 0 feet) if you move at least 10 f...

text.text: When you make a High Jump, you leap into the air a number of feet equal to 3 plus your Strength modifier (minimum of 0 feet) if you move at least 10 feet on foot immediately before the jump. When you make a standing High Jump, you can jump only half that distance. Either way, each foot of the jump costs a foot of movement.
You can extend your arms half your height above yourself during the jump. Thus, you can reach a distance equal to the height of the jump plus 11/2 times your height.

Untrained hirelings are hired for work that requires no particular proficiencies; they include laborers and porters.

docs.title: Identifying a Magic Item
docs.excerpt: No excerpt available.

text.text: Some magic items are indistinguishable from their nonmagical counterparts, while others are conspicuously magical. Handling a magic item is enough to give you a sense that it is extraordinary, but learning a magic item's properties isn't automatic.

The Identify spell is the fastest way to reveal an item's properties. Alternatively, you can focus on one magic item during a Short Rest while being in physical contact with the item. At the end of the rest, you learn its properties and how to use them (but not any curse the item might bear).
Sometimes a magic item carries a clue to its properties. The command word to activate a ring might be etched inside the band, or a feathered design might hint that it's a Ring of Feather Falling.

Wearing or experimenting with an item can also offer hints about its properties. In the specific case of Potions, a little taste is enough to tell the taster what a potion does. Other items might require more experimentation. For example, if your character puts on a Ring of Swimming, the game might indicate that your movement feels strangely fluid. Perhaps you then dive into a river to see what happens, and the game confirms that you swim unexpectedly well.

docs.title: Illusions
docs.excerpt: Spells and other effects sometimes create magical illusions. Such an effect defines what the illusion does and which senses or mental faculties it dec...

text.text: Spells and other effects sometimes create magical illusions. Such an effect defines what the illusion does and which senses or mental faculties it deceives.

If an illusion manifests in space, the illusion is insubstantial and weightless, yet it seems to be affected by the environment as if the illusion were real unless the effect that created it specifies otherwise. For example, a visual illusion of a creature casts shadows and reflections, and wind appears to affect the illusory creature. Similarly, an audible illusion echoes in an echoey space.

docs.excerpt: An improvised weapon is an object wielded as a makeshift weapon, such as broken glass, a table leg, or a frying pan. A Simple or Martial weapon also c...

text.text: An improvised weapon is an object wielded as a makeshift weapon, such as broken glass, a table leg, or a frying pan. A Simple or Martial weapon also counts as an improvised weapon if it's wielded in a way contrary to its design; if you use a Ranged weapon to make a melee attack or throw a Melee weapon that lacks the Thrown property, the weapon counts as an improvised weapon. An improvised weapon follows the rules below.
Don't add your
to attack rolls with an improvised weapon.
On a hit, the weapon deals 1d4 damage of a type the DM thinks is appropriate for the object.
If you throw the weapon, it has a normal range of 20 feet and a long range of 60 feet.
If an improvised weapon resembles a Simple or Martial weapon, the DM may say it functions as that weapon and uses that weapon's rules. For example, the DM could treat a table leg as a Club.

docs.excerpt: Whenever an uncontrolled fire spreads, it can create an inferno that quickly engulfs creatures, objects, and vegetation. An inferno consists of at lea...

text.text: Whenever an uncontrolled fire spreads, it can create an inferno that quickly engulfs creatures, objects, and vegetation.
An inferno consists of at least four contiguous 10-foot

of fire. Each of the inferno's 10-foot Cubes can be doused with 10 gallons of water. Exposure to a strong wind for 1 minute causes the inferno to grow, adding 1d4 new 10-foot Cubes to its volume. An inferno deprived of fuel burns itself out after 1d10 minutes.

An inferno damages any vegetation or object that isn't being worn or carried that it touches, dealing 22 (4d10) Fire damage immediately and again at the end of each minute. Any creature that enters the inferno for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there takes 22 (4d10) Fire damage and is burning.

docs.title: Influence
docs.excerpt: With the Influence action, you urge a monster to do something. Describe or roleplay how you're communicating with the monster. Are you trying to decei...

text.text: With the Influence action, you urge a monster to do something. Describe or roleplay how you're communicating with the monster. Are you trying to deceive, intimidate, amuse, or gently persuade? The DM then determines whether the monster feels willing, unwilling, or hesitant due to your interaction; this determination establishes whether an ability check is necessary, as explained below.
If your urging aligns with the monster's desires, no ability check is necessary; the monster fulfills your request in a way it prefers.
If your urging is repugnant to the monster or counter to its alignment, no ability check is necessary; it doesn't comply.

If you urge the monster to do something that it is hesitant to do, you must make an ability check, which is affected by the monster's attitude: Indifferent, Friendly, or Hostile, each of which is defined in this glossary. The Influence Checks table suggests which ability check to make based on how you're interacting with the monster. The DM chooses the check, which has a default DC equal to 15 or the monster's Intelligence score, whichever is higher. On a successful check, the monster does as urged. On a failed check, you must wait 24 hours (or a duration set by the DM) before urging it in the same way again.
Influence Checks

rows.text: Deceiving a monster that understands you
Persuading a monster that understands you
Gently coaxing a Beast or Monstrosity

docs.title: Influence [Action]
docs.excerpt: A creature uses the Influence action to apply deliberate social pressure, persuasion, leverage, or presence in an attempt to change another creature's disposition, decision state, or willingness to cooperate.

text.text: The Influence action is a deliberate attempt to change another creature's disposition or decision state through words, tone, authority, leverage, presence, or social pressure. It is used when the acting creature is not forcing the outcome through direct damage or control, but is instead trying to shift what another creature is willing to allow, reveal, accept, or do.
Rules
A creature uses its
to make a focused social attempt against another creature or group
Influence is about changing cooperation, resistance, disclosure, or immediate decision state
The current relationship between the creatures matters to how much influence is possible
Existing fear, loyalty, hostility, indifference, obligation, and leverage can all change how the action resolves
Relationship Pressure

Influence operates on disposition and willingness. A creature that is already aligned, friendly, or uncertain may be easier to move toward cooperation, while a creature that is actively hostile, protected by strong loyalty, or committed to an opposing goal may resist even a strong attempt.
Scope

Influence does not automatically create lasting friendship or rewrite a creature's core nature. It resolves a social pressure event in the current situation. The result may be cooperation, hesitation, limited disclosure, a softened stance, or no change at all depending on the governing rule and the target's state.
Common Uses
Convincing a creature to allow passage, offer aid, or delay aggression
Pressuring a witness, guard, or negotiator to reveal information
Shifting a creature from resistance toward temporary cooperation
Testing whether another creature can be swayed without magic or violence

docs.excerpt: Summarizes the cost of living, from Wretched to Aristocratic.

text.text: Lifestyle expenses summarize the cost of living in a fantasy world. They cover lodging, food, equipment maintenance, and other necessities.

At the start of each week or month (based on game settings), choose a lifestyle below-Wretched, Squalid, Poor, Modest, Comfortable, Wealthy, or Aristocratic-and pay the price to sustain that lifestyle.
Lifestyles have no inherent consequences, but the game might take them into account when determining risks or how others perceive your character.

That extra attack must be made with a different Light weapon, and you don't add your ability modifier to the extra attack's damage unless that modifier is negative. For example, you can attack with a Shortsword in one hand and a Dagger in the other using the Attack action and a Bonus Action, but you don't add your Strength or Dexterity modifier to the damage roll of the Bonus Action unless that modifier is negative.

docs.title: Line
docs.excerpt: A line is a three-dimensional area of effect that projects outward from a point of origin in a straight path of a specified length and width.

text.text: A line is a three-dimensional area of effect that extends in a straight path from a point of origin. It represents a concentrated vector or narrow beam, commonly used for lightning bolts, energy beams, gusts of wind, or projectile paths.
Geometry and Projections
Straight Path
: The effect begins at a point of origin and extends in a single straight path along its length.
Sizing Parameters

: The effect specifying the line defines both its length (how far it extends) and its width (how wide the path is). For example, a common line effect might extend 100 feet in length and be 5 feet wide.
Uniform Volume
: Unlike a cone, which widens as it extends, a line maintains a uniform width throughout its entire length.
Origin and Spatial Interaction
Origin Tip
: The point of origin sits at the beginning of the line's path.
Origin Exclusion

: The point of origin is not included in the line's area of effect unless its creator decides otherwise. This ensures the caster or launching mechanism at the origin is not affected by the beam projected from their position.
Tactical Applications

Lines are highly precise, making them perfect for piercing columns of enemies, cutting through narrow corridors, or sniping distant single targets across a crowded battlefield. However, because their width is usually very narrow (often only 5 feet), slight changes in target positions or terrain blockages can easily reduce the number of targets affected compared to wider radial shapes.

docs.title: Lockpicking
docs.excerpt: No excerpt available.

text.text: Lockpicking is the art of opening locks without the original key. This requires Thieves' Tools and is typically performed using a Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) check or a straight Dexterity check, depending on the game.
Making the Attempt
Check:
Dexterity + Proficiency Bonus (if proficient with Thieves' Tools)
DC:
Set by the lock's quality (typically 10-25)
Time:
Usually 1 minute; can attempt as an action with disadvantage
Outcomes
Success:
The lock opens
Failure:
The lock remains closed; you may try again
Critical Failure (5+ below DC):
If trapped, the trap triggers
Alternative: Forcing the Lock
Locks can also be broken using a Strength check against the lock's Break DC. This is louder and may damage the lock permanently, but doesn't require Thieves' Tools.

This Long Jump rule assumes that the height of the jump doesn't matter, such as a jump across a stream or chasm. At your DM's option, you must succeed on a DC 10 Strength (

During sleep, you have the
condition. After you finish a Long Rest, you must wait at least 16 hours before starting another one.
To start a Long Rest, you must have at least 1
. When you finish the rest, you gain the following benefits:
Regain All HP:
You regain all lost
and all spent
. If your Hit Point maximum was reduced, it returns to normal.
Ability Scores Restored:
If any of your ability scores were reduced, they return to normal.
Exhaustion Reduced:
If you have the
condition, its level decreases by 1.
Special Feature:
Some features are recharged by a Long Rest. If you have such a feature, it recharges in the way specified in its description.
Interrupting the Rest
A Long Rest is stopped by the following interruptions:
Rolling
Casting a spell other than a cantrip
Taking any damage.
1 hour of walking or other physical exertion.
If you rested at least 1 hour before the interruption you gain the benefits of a
You can resume a Long Rest immediately after an interruption. If you do so, the rest requires 1 additional hour per interruption to finish.

text.text: A mount can help you move more quickly through the wilderness, but its primary purpose is to carry gear that would otherwise slow you down. The Mounts and Other Animals table shows each animal's carrying capacity. See creature stat blocks for the animals' stat blocks.

docs.title: One Thing at a Time
docs.excerpt: The game uses actions to govern how much you can do at one time. You can take only one action at a time. This principle is most important in combat.

text.text: The game uses actions to govern how much you can do at one time. You can take only one action at a time. This principle is most important in combat, as explained in the Combat section later in this chapter.

Actions can come up in other situations, too: in a social interaction, you can try to Influence a creature or use the Search action to read the creature's body language, but you can't do both at the same time. And when you're exploring a dungeon, you can't simultaneously use the Search action to look for traps and use the Help action to aid another character who's trying to open a stuck door (with the Utilize action).

docs.title: Pact of the Blade
docs.excerpt: As a bonus_action|Bonus Action, you can conjure a pact weapon in your hand—a Simple or Martial Melee weapon of your choice with which you bond—or crea...

text.text: As a

, you can conjure a pact weapon in your hand—a Simple or Martial Melee weapon of your choice with which you bond—or create a bond with a magic weapon you touch; you can't bond with a magic weapon if someone else is attuned to it or another Warlock is bonded with it. Until the bond ends, you have proficiency with the weapon, and you can use it as a Spellcasting Focus.

Whenever you attack with the bonded weapon, you can use your Charisma modifier for the attack and damage rolls instead of using Strength or Dexterity; and you can cause the weapon to deal Necrotic, Psychic, or Radiant damage or its normal damage type.
Your bond with the weapon ends if you use this feature's
again, if the weapon is more than 5 feet away from you for 1 minute or more, or if you die. A conjured weapon disappears when the bond ends.

action, you can forgo one of your own attacks to allow your familiar to make one attack of its own with its

or

When the book appears, choose three cantrips, and choose two level 1 spells that have the

tag. The spells can be from any class's spell list, and they must be spells you don't already have prepared. While the book is on your person, you have the chosen spells prepared, and they function as Warlock spells for you.
You can use the book as a Spellcasting Focus.

on such checks, increase the score by 5. If the creature has
on them, decrease the score by 5. For example, a level 1 character with a Wisdom of 15 and proficiency in Perception has a Passive Perception of 14 (10 + 2 + 2). If that character has

docs.title: Planar Effects
docs.excerpt: Certain planes of existence—and areas on the Material Plane affected by them—have supernatural effects on creatures within them. Several examples are ...

text.text: Certain planes of existence—and areas on the Material Plane affected by them—have supernatural effects on creatures within them. Several examples are presented below. See chapter 6 for more information about the planes of existence.
Acheronian Bloodlust
The plane of Acheron rewards a creature for harming other creatures by imbuing it with the strength to keep fighting. While in Acheron or a region under its influence, a creature gains
equal to half its Hit Point maximum whenever it reduces another creature to 0 Hit Points.
Arcadian Vitality
On the plane of Arcadia and in regions where its influence is felt, creatures benefit from supernatural vitality, which grants them
the
and
conditions.
Blessed Beneficence

On the plane of Mount Celestia and in regions where its influence is felt, creatures are blessed by the pervasive beneficence of the plane. Creatures other than Fiends and Undead gain the benefit of the
spell as long as they remain in an area of blessed beneficence. In addition, such a creature that finishes a Long Rest in the area gains the benefit of a
spell.
Gehennan Cruelty

On the plane of Gehenna and in regions where its influence is felt, creatures are driven to loathe helping one another. Whenever a creature subject to Gehennan cruelty casts a spell, including a spell that restores Hit Points or removes a condition other than
, the caster must succeed on a DC 10 Charisma saving throw, or the spell fails and is wasted.
Winds of Pandemonium

The winds of Pandemonium are common in Pandemonium and regions where its influence is felt, including parts of the Underdark. A creature makes a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw after each hour spent among the howling winds. On a failed save, the creature gains 1
level. The winds can't increase a creature's
level above 3. Finishing a Long Rest doesn't reduce a creature's Exhaustion level unless the creature can somehow escape the winds.

text.text: Poisonous gas is usually encountered in an enclosed space, such as a sewer or sealed tomb. The gas fills as much space as it can, up to a maximum of ten 10-foot
. The transparent gas carries a foul odor. The gas is continuously or periodically replenished by some natural or magical source, though a strong wind disperses the gas for 1 minute.

Any creature that enters poisonous gas for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there makes a DC 12 Constitution saving throw, taking 5 (1d10) Poison damage on a failed save or half as much damage on a successful one. Creatures in the gas also have
on

docs.title: Proficiency Bonus
docs.excerpt: How to add your Proficiency Bonus to an attack roll.

text.text: You add your Proficiency Bonus to your attack roll when you attack using a weapon you have proficiency with, as well as when you attack with a spell. See the Proficiency entry later in this chapter for more information about weapon proficiencies.

docs.title: Proficiency Bonus
docs.excerpt: How to add your Proficiency Bonus to an ability check.

text.text: Add your Proficiency Bonus to an ability check when the game determines that a skill or tool proficiency is relevant to the check and you have that proficiency. For example, if a rule refers to a Strength (Acrobatics or Athletics) check, you can add your Proficiency Bonus to the check if you have proficiency in the Acrobatics or Athletics skill. See the Proficiency entry later in this chapter for more information about skill and tool proficiencies.

docs.title: Quicksand Pit
docs.excerpt: A quicksand pit is 10 feet deep and covers the ground in a 10-foot square. A creature that enters the area sinks 1d4 + 1 feet into the quicksand and h...

text.text: A quicksand pit is 10 feet deep and covers the ground in a 10-foot square. A creature that enters the area sinks 1d4 + 1 feet into the quicksand and has the

condition. At the start of each of the creature's turns, it sinks another 1d4 feet. As long as the creature isn't completely submerged in quicksand, it can take an action to try to escape, doing so with a successful Strength (
) check (DC 10 plus the number of feet the creature has sunk into the quicksand). A creature that is completely submerged in quicksand has Cover and the
condition and risks suffocation.
A creature can pull another creature within its reach out of a quicksand pit by taking an action and succeeding on a Strength (
) check (DC 5 plus the number of feet the creature has sunk into the quicksand).
At Higher Levels
You can scale this hazard for higher levels by making the pit deeper and increasing the number of feet a creature sinks at the start of each of its turns, as shown in the following table.

The first is the weapon's normal range in feet, and the second is the weapon's long range. When attacking a target beyond normal range, you have Disadvantage on the attack roll. You can't attack a target beyond the long range.

docs.title: Raw Materials
docs.excerpt: No excerpt available.

text.text: To make an item, you need raw materials worth half its purchase cost (round down). For example, you need 750 GP of raw materials to make Plate Armor, which sells for 1,500 GP. The game determines whether appropriate raw materials are available.

docs.title: Razorvine
docs.excerpt: Razorvine is a plant that grows in wild tangles and hedges. It also clings to the sides of buildings and other surfaces as ivy does. A 10-foot-high, 1...

text.text: Razorvine is a plant that grows in wild tangles and hedges. It also clings to the sides of buildings and other surfaces as ivy does. A 10-foot-high, 10-foot-wide, 5-foot-thick wall or hedge of razorvine has AC 11; HP 25; and
to Bludgeoning, Piercing, and Psychic damage.
When a creature comes into contact with razorvine for the first time on a turn, the creature must succeed on a DC 10 Dexterity saving throw or take 5 (1d10) Slashing damage from the bladelike thorns.

docs.title: Reach
docs.excerpt: Reach is the distance from which a creature can affect a target with a close-quarters attack or similar direct interaction.

text.text: Reach is the distance from which a creature can directly affect a nearby
with a close-quarters attack or another contact-based interaction.
Rules Use
Reach determines whether a target is close enough for a melee attack, a grab, a physical strike, or another effect that depends on being adjacent or nearly adjacent.
Changing Reach
A creature's normal reach can be extended by a weapon, anatomy, effect, size relationship, or another rule that changes how far its close-range influence extends.
Related Concepts
is one common rule that extends reach through a weapon
depend on whether another creature leaves the range your reach currently covers

docs.title: Reach
docs.excerpt: No excerpt available.

text.text: A Reach weapon adds 5 feet to your reach when you attack with it, as well as when determining your reach for Opportunity Attacks with it.

docs.title: Reaction
docs.excerpt: A Reaction is a special action taken in response to a trigger defined in the Reaction's description. You can take a Reaction on another creature's tur...

text.text: A Reaction is a special action taken in response to a trigger defined in the Reaction's description. You can take a Reaction on another creature's turn, and if you take it on your turn, you can do so even if you also take an action, a
, or both. Once you take a Reaction, you can't take another one until the start of your next turn. The
is a Reaction available to all creatures.

docs.title: Reactions
docs.excerpt: An instant response to a trigger of some kind, which can occur on your turn or on someone else's.

text.text: Certain special abilities, spells, and situations allow you to take a special action called a Reaction. A Reaction is an instant response to a trigger of some kind, which can occur on your turn or on someone else's. The
, described later in this chapter, is the most common type of Reaction.

When you take a Reaction, you can't take another one until the start of your next turn. If the reaction interrupts another creature's turn, that creature can continue its turn right after the Reaction.
In terms of timing, a Reaction takes place immediately after its trigger unless the Reaction's description says otherwise.

docs.title: Ready
docs.excerpt: You take the Ready action to wait for a particular circumstance before you act. To do so, you take this action on your turn, which lets you act by tak...

text.text: You take the Ready action to wait for a particular circumstance before you act. To do so, you take this action on your turn, which lets you act by taking a
before the start of your next turn.
First, you decide what perceivable circumstance will trigger your
. Then, you choose the action you will take in response to that trigger, or you choose to move up to your
in response to it. Examples include "If the cultist steps on the trapdoor, I'll pull the lever that opens it," and "If the zombie steps next to me, I move away."
When the trigger occurs, you can either take your
right after the trigger finishes or ignore the trigger.
When you Ready a spell, you cast it as normal (expending any resources used to cast it) but hold its energy, which you release with your

when the trigger occurs. To be readied, a spell must have a casting time of an action, and holding on to the spell's magic requires Concentration, which you can maintain up to the start of your next turn. If your Concentration is broken, the spell dissipates without taking effect.

docs.title: Ready [Action]
docs.excerpt: A creature uses the Ready action to hold part of its turn in reserve, define a trigger, and resolve the prepared response later as a reaction if that trigger occurs before the start of its next turn.

text.text: The Ready action delays part of a creature's turn so it can respond to a later event. Instead of resolving an intended action immediately, the creature defines a trigger and holds a specific response in reserve.
Rules
A creature uses its
to ready a response
The creature defines a perceivable trigger that will determine when the held response can resolve
The creature also defines the response it intends to take when that trigger occurs
If the trigger occurs before the start of the creature's next turn, the held response resolves as a
If the trigger never occurs in time, the held response expires without resolving
Trigger And Resolution

A readied response depends on an external event entering the game state. The trigger should be specific enough that the system can tell when the held response becomes available. Once the trigger occurs, the creature can spend its reaction to execute the reserved response at that moment instead of waiting for its next turn.
Timing Tradeoff

Readying an action exchanges immediate certainty for later timing control. The creature spends its action now, but the result is delayed and still depends on retaining its reaction until the trigger occurs.
Common Uses
Hold an attack until a target leaves cover
Move when a doorway opens or a bridge lowers
Respond to an enemy crossing a guarded position
Release a prepared spell or ability when a specific condition is met
Limits
The Ready action does not create an extra action. It repositions when a defined response can occur, and that delayed response still competes for the creature's reaction in the current round.

docs.title: Ritual
docs.excerpt: If you have a spell prepared that has the Ritual tag, you can cast that spell as a Ritual. The Ritual version of a spell takes 10 minutes longer to ca...

text.text: If you have a spell prepared that has the Ritual tag, you can cast that spell as a Ritual. The Ritual version of a spell takes 10 minutes longer to cast than normal. It also doesn't expend a spell slot, which means the ritual version of a spell can't be cast at a higher level.

docs.title: River Styx
docs.excerpt: The River Styx courses through the Lower Planes. Tasting or touching its waters can shatter a creature's intellect and personality, as well as strip a...

text.text: The River Styx courses through the Lower Planes. Tasting or touching its waters can shatter a creature's intellect and personality, as well as strip away its memories. Certain Fiends are immune to the river's effects.

Unless immune to the river's effects, a creature that drinks from the Styx, enters the river, or starts its turn in the river makes a DC 20 Intelligence saving throw. On a failed save, the creature takes 19 (3d12) Psychic damage and can't cast spells or take the
action for 30 days. An affected creature can drink from the Styx and swim in its waters without suffering any additional effects.
The effect can be ended only by the
, or
spell. If the effect isn't ended after 30 days, the effect becomes permanent and the creature loses all its memories. At that point, nothing short of a
spell or divine intervention can undo the effect.

Water taken from the River Styx loses its potency after 24 hours, becoming a harmless, foul-tasting liquid. Arcanaloths, night hags, and other fell creatures might know rituals that can prolong the water's potency, at your discretion.

docs.title: Rockslide
docs.excerpt: When a rockslide occurs, every creature in its path makes a DC 15 Dexterity saving throw. On a failed save, a creature takes 11 (2d10) Bludgeoning dam...

text.text: When a rockslide occurs, every creature in its path makes a DC 15 Dexterity saving throw. On a failed save, a creature takes 11 (2d10) Bludgeoning damage, has the
condition, and moves with the rockslide. On a successful save, a creature takes half as much damage only.
When the rockslide stops, its space becomes
, and all Prone creatures in its space are buried under rocks and debris. A creature buried in this way has the
condition and has Cover. As an action, a creature can try to crawl out from under the rock pile. If the creature succeeds on a DC 15 Strength (
) check, it is no longer Restrained; instead, it has the
condition and is atop the rock pile. If the check fails, the creature remains buried and gains 1
level.
A creature that has neither the Incapacitated nor the Restrained condition can spend 1 minute freeing another creature buried by the rockslide.
At Higher Levels
You can scale this hazard for higher levels by increasing the Bludgeoning damage as follows: 22 (4d10) at levels 5–10, 55 (10d10) at levels 11–16, and 99 (18d10) at levels 17–20.

docs.title: Roll Two D20s
docs.excerpt: How to roll with Advantage or Disadvantage.

text.text: When a roll has either Advantage or Disadvantage, roll a second d20 when you make the roll. Use the higher of the two rolls if you have Advantage, and use the lower roll if you have Disadvantage. For example, if you have Disadvantage and roll an 18 and a 3, use the 3. If you instead have Advantage and roll those numbers, use the 18.

docs.title: Rolling 20 or 1
docs.excerpt: The special rules for rolling a natural 20 or natural 1 on an attack roll.

text.text: If you roll a 20 on the d20 (called a "natural 20") for an attack roll, the attack hits regardless of any modifiers or the target's AC. This is called a Critical Hit.
If you roll a 1 on the d20 (a "natural 1") for an attack roll, the attack misses regardless of any modifiers or the target's AC.

text.text: Whenever you divide or multiply a number in the game, round down if you end up with a fraction, even if the fraction is one-half or greater. Some rules make an exception and tell you to round up.

text.text: A saving throw-also called a save-represents an attempt to avoid or resist a threat. You normally make a saving throw only when a rule requires you to do so, but you can decide to fail the save without rolling. The result of a save is detailed in the effect that allowed it. If a target is forced to make a save and lacks the ability score used by it, the target automatically fails.

docs.title: Search [Action]
docs.excerpt: A creature uses the Search action to actively gather information from its surroundings, looking for hidden creatures, concealed objects, tracks, weak points, or other details that are not immediately obvious.

text.text: The Search action represents deliberate information gathering. A creature slows down enough to inspect a space, study a target, listen for movement, or test whether something hidden is present.
Rules
A creature uses its
to make an active attempt to detect something not already obvious
Search is used when the current scene contains uncertainty that can be reduced through focused attention or closer examination
The relevant check is usually tied to noticing, interpreting, or uncovering details in the environment rather than directly changing it
A successful search reveals information the creature could not reliably act on before the search resolved
What Search Can Reveal
A Search action can help expose:
hidden or concealed creatures
secret doors, compartments, or disguised openings
traps, clues, tracks, and disturbances
faint sounds, movement, or other signs of presence
details that distinguish illusion, cover, concealment, or misdirection from the underlying reality
Active Detection
Search is an active alternative to relying on passive awareness alone. It represents a creature committing time and attention to reduce ambiguity in the current situation.
Search And Hidden Targets

A hidden or concealed target is not automatically found just because it exists in the scene. Search is one of the main ways a creature can deliberately test whether something unseen is nearby and refine its understanding of where that target actually is.
Related Concepts
reflects ongoing background awareness without spending an action
] creates the kind of uncertainty that Search often tries to overcome
can prevent ordinary sight from resolving a target clearly, which may force other forms of detection

You can use Seeking Spell even if you've already used a different Metamagic option during the casting of the spell.

, spells, curses, and the like-carry over from one form to the other. You revert to your true form if you die.

docs.title: Shape-Shifting
docs.excerpt: Shape-shifting is a transformation state in which magic or another effect changes a creature's body while the world continues resolving that creature through the altered form for the effect's duration.

text.text: Shape-shifting is a transformation state that changes a creature's body into another form or materially rewrites that body's outward structure. While the effect persists, the world resolves the creature through the altered form rather than through the prior one wherever the governing effect says that change matters.
Rules Use
Shape-shifting changes how a creature currently resolves in the world-state
The creature remains the subject of the effect even when its body, profile, or movement expression changes
The effect defines which parts of the creature carry over and which parts are replaced by the new form

title.text: Statistics Can Change With The Form

text.text: A shape-shifting effect can change a creature's
, movement profile, natural attacks, senses, or other embodied capabilities
Some effects also change or constrain
, while others leave that classification intact
The specific effect determines whether equipment merges, drops, resizes, or remains usable
Many shape-shifting effects persist only while their stated duration continues
If the effect requires
, the altered form ends when that concentration breaks
When the effect ends, the creature returns to whatever state the ending rule specifies instead of remaining indefinitely in the temporary form
Resolution Meaning

Shape-shifting is not only cosmetic. It can reframe targeting, movement, survivability, environmental access, and what body the world currently treats as present. Any question about the transformed creature should be answered from the active effect's text first.

text.text: Repairs to a damaged ship can be made while the vessel is berthed. Repairing 1 Hit Point of damage requires 1 day and costs 20 GP for materials and labor. If the repairs are made in a location where supplies and skilled labor are abundant, such as a city shipyard, the repair time and cost are halved.

When you finish the rest, you gain the following benefits:
Spend Hit Point Dice:

to regain

you spend in this way, roll the die and add your Constitution modifier to it. You regain
equal to the total (minimum of 1 Hit Point). You can decide to spend an additional
after each roll.
Special Feature:
Some features are recharged by a Short Rest. If you have such a feature, it recharges in the way specified in its description.
A Short Rest is stopped by the following interruptions:
Rolling
Casting a spell other than a cantrip
Taking any damage
An interrupted Short Rest confers no benefits.

docs.title: Skill Proficiencies

text.text: Most
involve using a skill, which represents a category of things creatures try to do with an
. The descriptions of the actions you take specify which skill applies if you make an
for that action, and many other rules note when a skill is relevant. The game has the ultimate say on whether a skill is relevant in a situation.
If a creature is proficient in a skill, the creature applies its
to
involving that skill. Without proficiency in a skill, a creature can still make
involving that skill but doesn't add its
. For example, if a character tries to climb a cliff, the game might ask for a Strength (Athletics) check. If the character has Athletics proficiency, the character adds their
to the Strength check. If the character lacks that proficiency, they make the check without adding their
The skills are shown on the Skills table, which notes example uses for each skill proficiency as well as the ability check the skill most often applies to.
