docs.title: Special Speeds
docs.excerpt: Rules for creatures that have multiple speeds, such as a walking speed and a flying speed, and how to switch between them during a move.

text.text: Some creatures have special speeds, such as a Burrow Speed, Climb Speed, Fly Speed, or Swim Speed, each of which is defined in this glossary. If you have more than one speed, choose which one to use when you move; you can switch between the speeds during your move. Whenever you switch, subtract the distance already moved from the new speed. The result determines how much farther you can move. If the result is 0 or less, you can't use the new speed during the current move. For example, if you have a
of 30 and a
of 40, you could fly 10 feet, walk 10 feet, and leap into the air to fly 20 feet more.

If the result is 0 or less, you can't use the new speed during the current move. For example, if you have a Speed of 30 and a

docs.title: Spell Slots
docs.excerpt: A limited resource that represents the magical energy you can expend to cast spells. Spell slots are expended when you cast a spell and are regained when you finish a long rest.

text.text: Regardless of how many spells a caster knows or prepares, they can cast only a limited number of spells before resting. Manipulating the fabric of magic and channeling its energy into even a simple spell is physically and mentally taxing, and higher-level spells are even more so.
Spell Slot Levels
Spell slots are divided into levels from 1st to 9th:
1st-level slots
: Cast 1st-level spells
2nd-level slots
: Cast 2nd-level spells
And so on up to 9th level
Using Spell Slots
When you cast a spell, you expend a spell slot of the spell's level or higher. For example:
To cast the 1st-level spell
Magic Missile
, you must expend a 1st-level slot
If you want to cast it using a 2nd-level slot, you can-the spell is cast at a higher level
Casting at Higher Levels
Some spells have enhanced effects when cast using a higher-level spell slot. For example:
Magic Missile
creates one additional dart for each slot level above 1st
Cure Wounds
heals more hit points when cast at higher levels
The spell's description tells you what happens when you cast it at a higher level.
Regaining Spell Slots
You regain all expended spell slots when you finish a
Warlocks
: Regain spell slots on a
Arcane Recovery
(Wizard): Recover some spell slots during a short rest
Sorcery Points
(Sorcerer): Can convert sorcery points to spell slots
Cantrips Don't Use Slots
are 0-level spells that don't require spell slots and can be cast at will.

docs.title: Spellcasting
docs.excerpt: Rules for hiring spellcasters.

text.text: Most settlements contain individuals who are willing to cast spells in exchange for payment. If a spell has expensive components, add the cost of those components to the cost listed in the Spellcasting Services table. The higher the level of a desired spell, the harder it is to find someone to cast it.

docs.title: Spells (Chapter Overview)
docs.excerpt: A general introduction to how spells function in the game, covering fundamental concepts. Detailed rules are found in sub-entries under 'Spellcasting Mechanics'.

text.text: This chapter gives rules for casting spells. It also includes descriptions of common spells in the worlds of Dungeons & Dragons. Those spells are used by many class features, magic items, and monsters.

docs.title: Sphere [Area of Effect]
docs.excerpt: A Sphere is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a point of origin outward in all directions. The effect that creates a Sphere specif...

text.text: A Sphere is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a point of origin outward in all directions. The effect that creates a Sphere specifies the distance it extends as the radius of the Sphere.
A Sphere's point of origin is included in the Sphere's area of effect.

docs.title: Strength
docs.excerpt: Measures physical power, athletic training, and the extent to which you can exert raw physical force. Strength is important for melee combat and lifting, pushing, or breaking things.

text.text: Strength measures bodily power, athletic training, and the extent to which you can exert raw physical force.
Strength Checks
A Strength check can model any attempt to lift, push, pull, or break something, to force your body through a space, or to otherwise apply brute force to a situation.
Your Strength (Athletics) check covers difficult situations you encounter while climbing, jumping, or swimming. Examples include:
Climbing a sheer or slippery cliff
Avoiding hazards while scaling a wall
Clinging to a surface while something tries to knock you off
Jumping an unusually long distance
Swimming or staying afloat in treacherous water
Forcing open
a stuck, locked, or barred door
Breaking free
of bonds
Pushing through
a tunnel that is too small
Hanging on
to a wagon while being dragged behind it
Tipping over
a statue
Keeping a boulder
from rolling
Attack Rolls and Damage
You add your Strength modifier to:
Attack rolls
when using melee weapons (unless it's a finesse weapon and you choose to use Dexterity)
Damage rolls
when attacking with a melee weapon
Attack and damage rolls
when throwing weapons (unless it's a weapon with the thrown property that you choose to use with Dexterity)
Carrying Capacity
Your carrying capacity is your Strength score multiplied by 15. This is the weight (in pounds) that you can carry.
Lifting and Pushing
You can push, drag, or lift a weight in pounds up to twice your carrying capacity (or 30 times your Strength score).

text.text: Strong wind imposes
on ranged attack rolls with weapons. It also extinguishes open flames and disperses fog. A flying creature in a strong wind must land at the end of its turn or fall.
A strong wind in a desert can create a sandstorm that imposes Disadvantage on Wisdom (
) checks.

docs.title: Study
docs.excerpt: When you take the Study action, you make an Intelligence check to study your memory, a book, a clue, or another source of knowledge and call to mind a...

text.text: When you take the Study action, you make an Intelligence check to study your memory, a book, a clue, or another source of knowledge and call to mind an important piece of information about it.
The Areas of Knowledge table suggests which skills are applicable to various areas of knowledge.
Areas of Knowledge

rows.text: Spells, magic items, eldritch symbols, magical traditions, planes of existence, and certain creatures (Aberrations, Constructs, Elementals, Fey, and Monstrosities)
Historic events and people, ancient civilizations, wars, and certain creatures (Giants and Humanoids)
Terrain, flora, weather, and certain creatures (Beasts, Dragons, Oozes, and Plants)
Deities, religious hierarchies and rites, holy symbols, cults, and certain creatures (Celestials, Fiends, and Undead)

docs.title: Study [Action]
docs.excerpt: The Study action is a deliberate attempt to analyze, identify, or interpret something in the scene. It is used when a creature spends focused effort to understand a creature, effect, object, clue, or other sign that is not resolved by a glance alone.

text.text: The Study action represents deliberate analysis. A creature uses it when it needs focused attention to identify a creature, interpret a magical effect, understand a sign, or extract meaning from information that is present but not yet understood.
Rules
A creature uses its
to study something that requires more than a quick look
Study is about interpretation and identification rather than broad scene scanning
The result usually depends on the kind of thing being examined, such as a creature, a symbol, a magical effect, a clue, or another unusual feature
Study often resolves through an ability check when the information is uncertain, hidden behind complexity, or gated by expertise
A successful study attempt can reveal properties, dangers, origins, patterns, weaknesses, or other rule-relevant details
What Study Is For

Study is the action for understanding what something is, how it works, or what it implies. It fits situations where the important question is not "where is it?" but "what is this?" or "what does this mean?"
Study Versus Search
Study and

are related but not identical. Search is about locating something that is concealed, subtle, or easy to miss. Study begins once the subject is already available to inspect and the challenge is to interpret it correctly.
Common Study Targets
A creature can study another creature to identify unusual traits, visible conditions, combat patterns, or other meaningful signs.
A creature can study a magical effect, enchantment, illusion, glyph, or magical residue to understand what kind of effect is present or what it is doing.
Tracks, written signs, ritual marks, damage patterns, and other environmental details can all become study targets when the question is about meaning rather than mere presence.
Resolution In Aralia

Aralia should treat Study as a focused information-gathering action. It converts time and attention into rule-relevant understanding, usually by resolving whether the acting creature can correctly analyze the chosen subject.

docs.title: Suffocation
docs.excerpt: A creature can hold its breath for a number of minutes equal to 1 plus its Constitution modifier (minimum of 30 seconds) before suffocation begins. Wh...

text.text: A creature can hold its breath for a number of minutes equal to 1 plus its Constitution modifier (minimum of 30 seconds) before suffocation begins. When a creature runs out of breath or is choking, it gains 1
level at the end of each of its turns. When a creature can breathe again, it removes all levels of
it gained from suffocating.

docs.title: Surprise
docs.excerpt: Surprise is a combat-start state that reflects an encounter beginning before one side is fully ready to respond. It affects how creatures enter the first round and whether they can act immediately when initiative begins.

text.text: Surprise is a combat-state determination made at the start of an encounter when not all combatants are equally prepared for immediate conflict. It is about readiness at initiative start, not an emotional reaction.
Rules
Surprise is resolved when combat begins and initiative order becomes relevant
A surprised creature enters the encounter from a disadvantaged readiness state during the opening exchange
The governing combat rule determines what actions, reactions, movement, or other responses are unavailable until that initial surprise state clears
Surprise depends on encounter awareness, detection, concealment, and timing rather than on allegiance alone
Encounter Start
A creature can be present in the scene and still be surprised if the encounter begins before it has a resolved opportunity to react as combat starts.
Concealment, hidden approach, misdirection, or failed detection can cause one side to begin combat without full readiness. Effects related to
, obscuration, or invisibility often influence whether surprise is possible.
Surprise matters when
begins. It changes how the first combat round opens rather than creating a separate encounter outside initiative.
Common Uses
An ambush beginning from concealment or obscured positioning
A hidden or unseen threat initiating combat before a target is ready
A sudden hostile transition in an encounter that had not yet resolved into open combat

If the original attack roll would hit the second creature, it takes damage equal to the number you roll on your Superiority Die. The damage is of the same type dealt by the original attack.

docs.title: Telepathy
docs.excerpt: Telepathy is a magical ability that allows a creature to communicate mentally with another creature within a specified range. Unless a rule states oth...

text.text: Telepathy is a magical ability that allows a creature to communicate mentally with another creature within a specified range. Unless a rule states otherwise, the contacted creature doesn't need to share a language with the telepath to understand this communication, but the contacted creature must be able to understand at least one language or be telepathic itself to understand.

A telepath doesn't need to see a contacted creature, and the telepath can start or end the telepathic contact at any time (no action required). Telepathic contact can't be initiated and is immediately broken if either the telepath or the other creature has the
condition. Telepathic contact is also broken if the contacted creature is no longer within the telepathy's range or if the telepath contacts a different creature within range.

A creature without telepathy can receive telepathic messages but can't initiate a telepathic conversation. Once a telepathic conversation starts, the non-telepath can communicate mentally to the telepath until the telepathic connection ends.

docs.title: Teleportation
docs.excerpt: Teleportation is a special kind of magical transportation. If you teleport, you disappear and reappear elsewhere instantly, without moving through the...

text.text: Teleportation is a special kind of magical transportation. If you teleport, you disappear and reappear elsewhere instantly, without moving through the intervening space. This transportation doesn't expend movement unless a rule tells you otherwise, and teleportation never provokes

When you teleport, all the equipment you're wearing and carrying teleports with you. If you're touching another creature when you teleport, that creature doesn't teleport with you unless the teleportation effect says otherwise.
If the destination space of your teleportation is occupied by another creature or blocked by a solid obstacle, you instead appear in the nearest unoccupied space of your choice.
The description of a teleportation effect tells you if you must see the teleportation's destination.

docs.title: Temporary Hit Points
docs.excerpt: A buffer of extra hit points that absorb damage before your actual hit points. Temporary hit points don't stack and aren't restored by healing.

text.text: Some spells and special abilities confer temporary hit points to a creature. Temporary hit points aren't actual
; they are a buffer against damage, a pool of hit points that protect you from injury.
Rules
Temporary hit points are
separate
from your actual hit points
When you take damage, temporary hit points are lost
first
Any leftover damage carries over to your normal hit points
If you have temporary hit points and receive more, you
don't add them together
Instead, you decide whether to keep the temporary hit points you have or gain the new ones
For example, if a spell grants you 12 temporary hit points when you already have 10, you can have 12 or 10, not 22
You can't regain temporary hit points through healing
Temporary hit points can't be added to your normal hit points
If you have 0 hit points, receiving temporary hit points doesn't restore you to consciousness
Unless a feature that grants you temporary hit points has a duration, they last until they're depleted or you finish a
Common Sources
Inspiring Leader
feat: Grant temporary HP to allies
False Life
spell: Grant yourself temporary HP
Heroism
spell: Grant temporary HP at the start of each turn
Armor of Agathys
spell: Gain temporary HP and deal cold damage to attackers
Fiendish Resilience
(Warlock): Gain resistance and temporary HP
Rage Beyond Death
(Barbarian): While raging at 0 HP, damage reduces temporary HP instead

docs.title: Temporary Hit Points
docs.excerpt: Temporary Hit Points are granted by certain effects and act as a buffer against losing real [[hit_points Points]]....

text.text: Temporary Hit Points are granted by certain effects and act as a buffer against losing real

docs.title: They Don't Stack
docs.excerpt: Rules for when multiple circumstances grant advantage or disadvantage.

text.text: If multiple situations affect a roll and they all grant Advantage on it, you still roll only two d20s. Similarly, if multiple situations impose Disadvantage on a roll, you roll only two d20s.

If circumstances cause a roll to have both Advantage and Disadvantage, the roll has neither of them, and you roll one d20. This is true even if multiple circumstances impose Disadvantage and only one grants Advantage or vice versa. In such a situation, you have neither Advantage nor Disadvantage.

docs.excerpt: Thin ice has a weight tolerance of 3d10 × 10 pounds per 10-foot-square area. Whenever the total weight on an area of thin ice exceeds its tolerance, t...

text.text: Thin ice has a weight tolerance of 3d10 × 10 pounds per 10-foot-square area. Whenever the total weight on an area of thin ice exceeds its tolerance, the ice in that area breaks. All creatures on broken ice fall through. Below the ice is frigid water.

action on your turn.

docs.title: Three-Quarters Cover
docs.excerpt: Three-quarters cover provides substantial protection when an obstacle screens at least three-quarters of a target's body.

text.text: Three-quarters cover represents significant physical screening by an intervening obstacle. It offers substantial protection against direct attacks and spatial hazards, leaving only a small portion of the target exposed.
Environmental Context

A target has three-quarters cover if an obstacle blocks at least three-quarters of its physical form from the source of an attack or effect. Examples include a portcullis, an arrow slit, a substantial boulder, or a thick castle wall screen.
Tactical Protection
provides a +5 bonus to
provides a +5 bonus to
saving throws
Targeting Dynamics

While the target remains visible and directly targetable, the narrow window of exposure makes landing a successful hit or affecting the target extremely challenging. It represents the final tier of partial exposure before transitioning into

docs.title: Time
docs.excerpt: No excerpt available.

text.text: To determine how many days (working 8 hours a day) it takes to make an item, divide its purchase cost in GP by 10 (round a fraction up to a day). For example, you need 5 days to make a Heavy Crossbow, which sells for 50 GP.
If an item requires multiple days, the days needn't be consecutive.

Characters can combine their efforts to shorten the crafting time. Divide the time needed to create an item by the number of characters working on it. Normally, only one other character can assist you, but the game might allow more assistants.

docs.title: Time-Limited Object Interactions
docs.excerpt: Rules for interacting with objects in combat.

text.text: When time is short, such as in combat, interactions with objects are limited: one free interaction per turn. That interaction must occur during a creature's movement or action. Any additional interactions require the Utilize action, as explained in "Combat" later in this chapter.

docs.title: Tool Proficiency
docs.excerpt: Rule for adding your Proficiency Bonus to checks using tools.

text.text: If you have proficiency with a tool, add your Proficiency Bonus to any ability check you make that uses the tool. If you have proficiency in a skill that's used with that check, you have Advantage on the check too.
Your features might give you proficiency with a tool. A monster has proficiency with any tool in its stat block.

docs.title: Tools
docs.excerpt: Rules for using tools for ability checks and crafting.

text.text: A tool helps you make specialized ability checks, craft certain items, or both. A tool's description includes the tool's cost and weight, as well as the following entries:
Ability.
This entry lists the ability to use when making an ability check with the tool.
Utilize.
This entry lists things you can do with the tool when you take the Utilize action. You can do one of those things each time you take the action. This entry also provides the DC for the action.
Craft.
This entry lists what, if anything, you can craft with the tool. For crafting rules, see "Crafting Equipment" later in the chapter.
Variants.
This entry appears if the tool has variants, which are listed. Each requires a separate proficiency.

docs.title: Total Cover
docs.excerpt: Total cover represents a complete physical barrier that blocks direct line of sight and target pathways between a source and a target.

text.text: Total cover represents a complete physical obstruction or opaque barrier standing between the source of an effect or attack and a target. It prevents direct targeting and blocks most physical and spatial trajectories.
Environmental Context

A target has total cover if it is completely screened by an intervening obstacle. Examples include a solid stone wall, a closed heavy wooden door, a thick rock pillar, or being positioned completely behind a substantial structure.
Tactical Protection
Direct Targeting Blocked
: A target with total cover cannot be targeted directly by attacks, spells, or other spatial effects that require a clear path or sightline.
Path Blocked
: The barrier physically interrupts direct vectors. Physical attacks, projectiles, and most magical projections fail to reach the target, resolving instead against the obstructing obstacle.
Exceptions and Interactions
While total cover blocks direct vectors, certain spatial effects can bypass it if they can wrap around corners or originate from an area of effect that extends past the barrier (such as certain
expansions like a
or
, provided the point of origin is positioned to reach behind the cover).
Scaling Cover
Total cover is the absolute tier of physical obstruction, succeeding
. It transitions back into partial cover if the target moves or if the barrier is damaged, destroyed, or breached.

docs.title: Transmuted Spell
docs.excerpt: When you cast a spell that deals a type of damage from the following list, you can spend 1 Sorcery Point to change that damage type to one of the othe...

text.text: When you cast a spell that deals a type of damage from the following list, you can spend 1 Sorcery Point to change that damage type to one of the other listed types: Acid, Cold, Fire, Lightning, Poison, Thunder.

docs.title: Traps
docs.excerpt: No excerpt available.

text.text: Traps are hidden dangers designed to harm or hinder intruders. They range from simple pit traps to complex magical wards. Adventurers may encounter traps on locked chests, doors, hallways, or any location where someone wants to protect their secrets.
Detecting Traps
Passive Detection:
Some traps can be noticed passively if your Passive Perception meets or exceeds the trap's Detection DC
Active Search:
Use an action to search for traps with a Wisdom (Perception) or Intelligence (Investigation) check
Magical Traps:
May require Arcana checks or the Detect Magic spell to notice
Disarming Traps
Mechanical Traps:
Dexterity check using Thieves' Tools against the trap's Disarm DC
Magical Traps:
Intelligence (Arcana) check, or may require Dispel Magic
Failure:
Failing by 5 or more may trigger the trap
Common Trap Types

docs.title: Travel
docs.excerpt: Rules for traveling long distances.

text.text: During an adventure, the characters might travel long distances on trips that could take hours or days. The game can summarize this travel without calculating exact distances or travel times, or the game might have you use the travel pace rules below. If you need to know how fast you can move when every second matters, see the movement rules in "Combat" later in this chapter.
Marching Order:

The adventurers should establish a marching order while they travel, whether indoors or outdoors. A marching order makes it easier to determine which characters are affected by traps, which ones can spot hidden enemies, and which ones are the closest to those enemies if a fight breaks out. You can change your marching order outside combat and record the order any way you like: write it down, for example, or arrange miniatures to show it.

docs.title: Travel
docs.excerpt: No excerpt available.

text.text: Drivers or crew hired to conduct passengers to their destinations charge the rates on the Travel table, plus any tolls or additional expenses.

docs.title: Travel Pace
docs.excerpt: Rules for Fast, Normal, and Slow travel paces.

text.text: While traveling outside combat, a group can move at a Fast, Normal, or Slow pace, as shown on the Travel Pace table. The table states how far the party can move in a period of time; if riding horses or other mounts, the group can move twice that distance for 1 hour, after which the mounts need a Short or Long Rest before they can move at that increased pace again (see " for a selection of mounts for sale). The " has rules that affect which pace you can choose in certain types of terrain.
Each travel pace has a game effect, as defined below.
Fast Pace
Imposes
on a traveler's
, and
checks.
Normal Pace
Imposes
on
checks.
Slow Pace
Grants
on
and
checks.

docs.title: Tremorsense
docs.excerpt: A creature with Tremorsense can pinpoint the location of creatures and moving objects within a specific range, provided that the creature with Tremors...

text.text: A creature with Tremorsense can pinpoint the location of creatures and moving objects within a specific range, provided that the creature with Tremorsense and anything it is detecting are both in contact with the same surface (such as the ground, a wall, or a ceiling) or the same liquid.
Tremorsense can't detect creatures or objects in the air, and it doesn't count as a form of sight.

condition.

Darkness:
You can see in normal and magical
Invisibility:
You see creatures and objects that have the
condition.
Visual Illusions:
Visual illusions appear transparent to you, and you automatically succeed on
against them.
Transformations:
You discern the true form of any creature or object you see that has been transformed by magic.
Ethereal Plane:
You see into the Ethereal Plane.

text.text: A Two-Handed weapon requires two hands when you attack with it.

docs.title: Unarmed Strike
docs.excerpt: Instead of using a weapon to make a melee attack, you can use a punch, kick, head-butt, or similar forceful blow. In game terms, this is an Unarmed St...

text.text: Instead of using a weapon to make a melee attack, you can use a punch, kick, head-butt, or similar forceful blow. In game terms, this is an Unarmed Strike-a melee attack that involves you using your body to damage, grapple, or shove a target within 5 feet of you.
Whenever you use your Unarmed Strike, choose one of the following options for its effect.
You make an attack roll against the target. Your bonus to the roll equals your Strength modifier plus your

The target must succeed on a Strength or Dexterity

docs.title: Utilize
docs.excerpt: You normally interact with an object while doing something else, such as when you draw a sword as part of the [[attack]] action. When an object requir...

text.text: You normally interact with an object while doing something else, such as when you draw a sword as part of the
action. When an object requires an action for its use, you take the Utilize action.

docs.title: Utilize [Action]
docs.excerpt: The Utilize action is a deliberate use of an object or interactable feature that requires more commitment than a free interaction. It covers operating, activating, manipulating, or applying something when that use meaningfully occupies the creature's action.

text.text: The Utilize action covers deliberate use of an object or an interactable system feature when the interaction is substantial enough to consume a creature's action. It applies when the creature is doing more than a quick free interaction and is instead operating, activating, applying, or manipulating something with meaningful intent.
Rules
A creature uses its
to utilize an object or feature that requires focused handling
Utilize is for interaction that goes beyond the small free interaction normally folded into movement or another action
The action can involve activating an item, applying a substance, operating a mechanism, or using an environmental feature that requires commitment and timing
If a rule gives an object, item, or feature its own activation procedure, that specific rule defines what the utilize attempt accomplishes
Utilize does not replace other actions with their own clearer rule identity, such as
or
What Counts As Utilize

Utilize is the right action when the important event is deliberate use of something external to the acting creature: a device, potion, tool, switch, lever, consumable, stored object, or comparable interactable feature.
Free Interaction Versus Utilize

Not every object interaction needs this action. Picking up a loose object, opening an ordinary door, or drawing a weapon may be handled by a simpler interaction rule. Utilize begins when the interaction becomes substantial enough that it should compete with attacking, casting, studying, or other action-level choices.
Common Utilize Targets
A creature might use Utilize to activate a device, administer an item, pour or apply a substance, or operate equipment that requires more than a momentary touch.
Levers, controls, ritual fixtures, locks, mechanisms, panels, and other environmental systems can all be utilize targets when operating them is a meaningful action choice.
Some consumables and special objects produce effects only when a creature spends the action needed to use them properly.
Resolution In Aralia

Aralia should treat Utilize as a deliberate interaction-action. It converts access to an object or feature into an attempted operation, with the final result determined by the specific item, system, or environmental rule being used.

text.text: Versatile
A Versatile weapon can be used with one or two hands. A damage value in parentheses appears with the property. The weapon deals that damage when used with two hands to make a melee attack.

docs.title: Vehicles
docs.excerpt: Rules for traveling in vehicles.

text.text: Travelers in wagons, carriages, or other land vehicles choose a pace as normal. Characters in a waterborne vessel are limited to the speed of the vessel, and they don't choose a travel pace. Depending on the vessel and the size of the crew, ships might be able to travel for up to 24 hours per day.
Equipment
includes vehicles for sale.

docs.title: Vicious Vine
docs.excerpt: Vicious vines are animated by magic. They often cling to doorways, archways, walls, and statuary. Until a vicious vine moves, there's nothing to disti...

text.text: Vicious vines are animated by magic. They often cling to doorways, archways, walls, and statuary. Until a vicious vine moves, there's nothing to distinguish it from an inanimate vine.

Each vicious vine has AC 11; HP 16; and Immunity to Bludgeoning, Piercing, and Psychic damage. When a creature enters a space within 5 feet of the vine for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there, the vine tries to coil around that creature. The target must succeed on a DC 12 Dexterity saving throw or have the

condition (escape DC 12). While Grappled in this way, the target takes 5 (1d10) Necrotic damage from the vine's life-draining thorns at the start of each of the target's turns. The vine can grapple only one creature at a time.
As an
action, a character under the effect of a
spell or similar magic can try to persuade the vine to release its victim, doing so with a successful DC 10 Charisma (
) check. A creature released by the vine in this way won't be attacked by that vine again for the next 24 hours.
At Higher Levels
You can scale the hazard for higher levels by increasing the Necrotic damage, the save DC, and the escape DC, as shown in the following table.

docs.title: Vision
docs.excerpt: Baseline rules for how creatures perceive the world through sight.

text.text: Vision is the primary way most creatures perceive their surroundings. A creature's ability to see is governed by the lighting conditions of its environment.
Creatures without special senses rely on
Normal Vision
. This baseline interaction with light determines what you can see and how well you can see it:

Disadvantage
on Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on sight.

Many creatures have traits that upgrade their vision, such as
, which allows them to ignore some of the penalties of low light. Other creatures possess
like Blindsight or Truesight that allow them to perceive the world without relying on light at all.

docs.title: Vision and Light
docs.excerpt: Rules governing sight and obscured areas.

text.text: Some adventuring tasks-such as noticing danger, hitting an enemy, and targeting certain spells-are affected by sight, so effects that obscure vision can hinder you, as explained in the
rules.
The environment's illumination level-

or

text.text: A weapon attack is an attack roll made with a weapon.

docs.title: Weapon Proficiency
docs.excerpt: Rule for adding your Proficiency Bonus to attack rolls with proficient weapons.

text.text: Anyone can wield a weapon, but you must have proficiency with it to add your Proficiency Bonus to an attack roll you make with it. A player character's features can provide weapon proficiencies. A monster is proficient with any weapon in its stat block.

docs.title: Weapons
docs.excerpt: Details on weapon categories, damage, properties, and mastery.

text.text: The Weapons table in this section shows the game's main weapons. The table lists the cost and weight of each weapon, as well as the following details:
Category.
Every weapon falls into a category: Simple or Martial. Weapon proficiencies are usually tied to one of these categories. For example, you might have proficiency with Simple weapons.
Melee or Ranged.
A weapon is classified as either Melee or Ranged. A Melee weapon is used to attack a target within 5 feet, whereas a Ranged weapon is used to attack at a greater distance.
Damage.
The table lists the amount of damage a weapon deals when an attacker hits with it as well as the type of that damage.
Properties.
Any properties a weapon has are listed in the Properties column. Each property is defined in the Properties section.
Mastery.
Each weapon has a mastery property, which is defined in the Mastery Properties section later in this chapter. To use that property, you must have a feature that lets you use it.

docs.title: Wearing and Wielding Items
docs.excerpt: Rules for how magic items are worn and used.

text.text: Using a magic item's properties might mean wearing or wielding it. A magic item meant to be worn must be donned in the intended fashion: boots go on feet, gloves on hands, hats and helmets on a head, and rings on a finger. Magic armor must be donned, a Shield strapped to the arm, a cloak fastened about the shoulders. A weapon must be held.
In most cases, a magic item that's meant to be worn can fit a creature regardless of size or build. Magic garments are made to be easily adjustable, or they magically adjust themselves to the wearer.

Each 10-foot
of webs has AC 10; HP 15; Vulnerability to Fire damage; and Immunity to Piercing, Poison, and Psychic damage.

docs.title: What is an Object?
docs.excerpt: Defining an object in game terms.

text.text: For the purpose of the rules, an object is a discrete, inanimate item like a window, door, sword, book, table, chair, or stone. It isn't a building or a vehicle, which are composed of many objects.

docs.title: Wild Magic Zone
docs.excerpt: A past magical disaster or uncontrolled surge of power in a particular place can cause the fabric of magic to unravel, creating a wild magic zone. In ...

text.text: A past magical disaster or uncontrolled surge of power in a particular place can cause the fabric of magic to unravel, creating a wild magic zone. In such an area, which is typically no more than 300 feet in diameter, magic behaves unpredictably.

Whenever a creature expends a spell slot to cast a spell in a wild magic zone, roll 1d20. If you roll a 20 on the die, roll on the Wild Magic Surge table in the Player's Handbook to create a magical effect.

docs.title: Wisdom
docs.excerpt: Measures awareness, intuition, and insight. Wisdom is important for clerics, druids, and rangers, and affects perception and many saving throws.

text.text: Wisdom reflects how attuned you are to the world around you and represents perceptiveness and intuition.
Wisdom Checks
A Wisdom check might reflect an effort to read body language, understand someone's feelings, notice things about the environment, or care for an injured person.
Your Wisdom (Animal Handling) check determines your ability to:
Calm down a domesticated animal
Keep a mount from getting spooked
Intuit an animal's intentions
Control your mount when you attempt a risky maneuver
Your Wisdom (Insight) check determines your ability to:
Determine the true intentions of a creature
Read body language
Understand someone's feelings
Detect lies or deception
Your Wisdom (Medicine) check determines your ability to:
Stabilize a dying companion
Diagnose an illness
Tend to wounds
Your Wisdom (Perception) check determines your ability to:
Spot, hear, or otherwise detect the presence of something
Notice hidden objects or creatures
Eavesdrop on a conversation
Hear combat in the distance
Your Wisdom (Survival) check determines your ability to:
Follow tracks
Hunt wild game
Guide your group through frozen wastelands
Identify signs that owlbears live nearby
Predict the weather
Avoid quicksand and other natural hazards
Spellcasting Ability
Wisdom is the spellcasting ability for:
Clerics
: Divine magic through faith and devotion
Druids
: Primal magic drawn from nature
Rangers
: Nature magic from their connection to the wild
Wisdom Saving Throws
Wisdom saving throws represent your ability to resist effects that would:
Charm
or dominate your mind
Frighten
you
Cause hallucinations
or illusions
Control your thoughts
or actions
Charm Person
Hold Person
Dominate Person
Fear
Hideous Laughter

text.text: Yellow mold grows in dark places, and one patch covers a 5-foot square. If touched, the mold ejects a cloud of spores that fills a 10-foot
. Any creature in that area must succeed on a DC 15 Constitution saving throw or take 11 (2d10) Poison damage and have the

condition for 1 minute. While Poisoned in this way, the creature takes 5 (1d10) Poison damage at the start of each of its turns. The creature repeats the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.
Direct sunlight or any amount of Fire damage destroys a patch of yellow mold.

docs.title: Casting Time Rules
docs.excerpt: Details the rules for spell casting times, including Magic action, Bonus Action, Reaction, longer casting times, and the one spell slot per turn rule.

text.text: Most spells require the
to cast, but some spells require a
, a
, or 1 minute or more. A spell's Casting Time entry specifies which of those is required.

title.text: One Spell with a Spell Slot per Turn

text.text: On a turn, you can expend only one
to cast a spell. This rule means you can't, for example, cast a spell with a
using the
and another one using a
on the same turn.
A spell that has a casting time of a
is cast in response to a trigger that is defined in the spell's Casting Time entry. Some spells that have a casting time of a
are also cast in response to a trigger defined in the spell.
Certain spells-including a spell cast as a
-require more time to cast: minutes or even hours. While you cast a spell with a casting time of 1 minute or more, you must take the
on each of your turns, and you must maintain
(see the
rule) while you do so. If your
is broken, the spell fails, but you don't expend a
. To cast the spell again, you must start over.

docs.title: Gaining and Preparing Spells
docs.excerpt: Details how characters learn new spells and prepare them for casting, including rules for always-prepared spells and changing prepared spells.

text.text: Before you can cast a spell, you must have the spell prepared in your mind or have access to the spell from a magic item, such as a Spell Scroll. Your features specify which spells you have access to, if any; whether you always have certain spells prepared; and whether you can change the list of spells you have prepared.

If you have a list of level 1+ spells you prepare, your spellcasting feature specifies when you can change the list and the number of spells you can change, as summarized in the Spell Preparation by Class table.
Spell Preparation by Class
Most spellcasting monsters don't change their lists of prepared spells, but the game is free to alter them.

Certain features might give you a spell that you always have prepared. If you also have a list of prepared spells that you can change, a spell that you always have prepared doesn't count against the number of spells on that list.
You must have training with any armor you are wearing to cast spells while wearing it. You are otherwise too hampered by the armor for spellcasting.

docs.title: Schools of Magic
docs.excerpt: Describes the eight schools of magic (Abjuration, Conjuration, etc.) that categorize spells based on their typical effects.

text.text: Each spell belongs to a school of magic. The schools are listed in the Schools of Magic table. These categories help describe spells but have no rules of their own, although some other rules refer to them.
Schools of Magic

rows.text: Channels energy to create effects that are often destructive

text.text: Class Spell Lists

If a spell is on a class's spell list, the class's name appears in parentheses after the spell's school of magic. Some features add a spell to a character's spell list even if the character isn't a member of a class in the parentheses.

docs.title: Spell Components (V, S, M)
docs.excerpt: Details the physical requirements for casting spells: Verbal (V), Somatic (S), and Material (M) components, including spellcasting focuses and component pouches.

text.text: A spell's components are physical requirements the spellcaster must meet to cast the spell. Each spell's description indicates whether it requires Verbal (V), Somatic (S), or Material (M) components. If the spellcaster can't provide one or more of a spell's components, the spellcaster can't cast the spell.

title.text: Verbal (V) A Verbal component is the chanting of esoteric words that sound like nonsense to the uninitiated. The words must be uttered in a normal speaking voice. The words themselves aren't the source of the spell's power; rather, the particular combination of sounds, with specific pitch and resonance, sets the threads of magic in motion. Thus, a creature who is gagged or in an area of magical silence can't cast a spell with a Verbal component. Creating Verbal Components If you'd like to say a spell's Verbal component, you may make up the words. However you make them, the goal is to create something that's easy to say and that doesn't mean anything in the real world. Consider this method: take the name of the spell, keep only one instance of each of its letters, and rearrange the remaining letters into words. For example, remove the second
and rearrange the remaining letters to create

. Somatic (S) A Somatic component is a forceful gesticulation or an intricate set of gestures. A spellcaster must use at least one of their hands to perform these movements. Material (M) A Material component is a particular material used in a spell's casting, as specified in parentheses in the Components entry. These materials aren't consumed by the spell unless the spell's description states otherwise. The spellcaster must have a hand free to access them, but it can be the same hand used to perform Somatic components, if any. If a spell doesn't consume its materials and doesn't specify a cost for them, a spellcaster can use a Component Pouch (see the Equipment section) instead of providing the materials specified in the spell, or the spellcaster can substitute a Spellcasting Focus if the caster has a feature that allows that substitution. To use a Component Pouch, you must have a hand free to reach into it, and to use a Spellcasting Focus, you must hold it unless its description says otherwise (see the Equipment section for descriptions).

docs.title: Spell Duration Rules
docs.excerpt: Explains how long a spell's effects persist, covering Concentration, Instantaneous durations, and fixed Time Spans (rounds, minutes, hours).

text.text: A spell's duration is the length of time the spell persists after it is cast. A duration typically takes one of the following forms:
A duration that requires
follows the
rules.
Instantaneous.
An instantaneous duration means the spell's magic appears only for a moment and then disappears.
Time Span.

A duration that provides a time span specifies how long the spell lasts in rounds, minutes, hours, or the like. For example, a Duration entry might say 1 minute, meaning the spell ends after 1 minute has passed. While a time-span spell that you cast is ongoing, you can dismiss it (no action required) if you don't have the

docs.title: Spell Effects (Targets, Saves, Attacks)
docs.excerpt: Details how spell effects are determined, including targeting rules (clear path, self, AoE), saving throws, attack rolls, and combining spell effects.

text.text: The effects of a spell are detailed after its duration entry. Those details present exactly what the spell does, which ignores mundane physical laws; any outcomes beyond those effects are under the game's purview. Whatever the effects, they typically deal with targets, saving throws, attack rolls, or all three, each of which is detailed below.
A typical spell requires the caster to pick one or more targets to be affected by the spell's magic. A spell's description says whether the spell targets creatures, objects, or something else.
A Clear Path to the Target.
To target something with a spell, a caster must have a clear path to it, so it can't be behind
Targeting Yourself.
If a spell targets a creature of your choice, you can choose yourself unless the creature must be
or specifically a creature other than you.
Areas of Effect.
Some spells, such as
, cover an area called an
. The area determines what the spell targets. The description of a spell specifies whether it has an area of effect, which is typically one of these shapes:
, or
Awareness of Being Targeted.

Unless a spell has a perceptible effect, a creature doesn't know it was targeted by the spell. An effect like lightning is obvious, but a more subtle effect, such as an attempt to read thoughts, goes unnoticed unless a spell's description says otherwise.
Invalid Targets.
If you cast a spell on someone or something that can't be affected by it, nothing happens to that target, but if you used a

to cast the spell, the slot is still expended. If the spell normally has no effect on a target that succeeds on a saving throw, the invalid target appears to have succeeded on its saving throw, even though it didn't attempt one (giving no hint that the creature is an invalid target). Otherwise, you perceive that the spell did nothing to the target.
Many spells specify that a target makes a
to avoid some or all of a spell's effects. The spell specifies the ability that the target uses for the save and what happens on a success or failure. Here's how to calculate the DC for your spells:
Spell save DC
= 8 + your spellcasting ability modifier + your
Some spells require the caster to make an
to determine whether the spell hits a target. Here's how to calculate the attack modifier for your spells:
Spell attack modifier
= your spellcasting ability modifier + your

The effects of different spells add together while their durations overlap. In contrast, the effects of the same spell cast multiple times don't combine. Instead, the most potent effect-such as the highest bonus-from those castings applies while their durations overlap. The most recent effect applies if the castings are equally potent and their durations overlap. For example, if two Clerics cast

on the same target, that target gains the spell's benefit only once; the target doesn't receive two bonus dice. But if the durations of the spells overlap, the effect continues until the duration of the second
Bless
ends.
Identifying an Ongoing Spell
You can try to identify a non-instantaneous spell by its observable effects if its duration is ongoing. To identify it, you must take the
and succeed on a DC 15 Intelligence (
) check.

docs.title: Spell Level & Slots
docs.excerpt: Explains spell levels as an indicator of power and spell slots as the resource for casting level 1+ spells, including rules for casting without slots and using higher-level slots.

text.text: Every spell has a level from 0 to 9, which is indicated in a spell's description. A spell's level is an indicator of how powerful it is. Cantrips-simple spells that can be cast almost by rote-are level 0. The rules for each spellcasting class say when its members gain access to spells of certain levels.
Spellcasting is taxing, so a spellcaster can cast only a limited number of level 1+ spells before resting.
are the main way a spellcaster's magical potential is represented. Each spellcasting class gives its members a limited number of
of certain spell levels. For example, a level 3 Wizard has four level 1
and two level 2 slots.
When you cast a spell, you expend a slot of that spell's level or higher, effectively filling a slot with the spell. Imagine a

is a groove of a certain size-small for a level 1 slot and larger for a higher-level spell. A level 1 spell fits into a slot of any size, but a level 2 spell fits only into a slot that's at least level 2. So when a level 3 Wizard casts
, a level 1 spell, that Wizard spends one of four level 1 slots and has three remaining.
Finishing a
restores any expended
There are several ways to cast a spell without expending a
Cantrips.
A cantrip is cast without a
Rituals.
Certain spells have the
tag in the
entry. Such a spell can be cast following the normal rules for spellcasting, or it can be cast as a
. The
version of a spell takes 10 minutes longer to cast than normal, but it doesn't expend a
. To cast a spell as a
, a spellcaster must have it prepared.
Special Abilities.
Some characters and monsters have special abilities that allow them to cast specific spells without a
. This casting is usually limited in another way, such as being able to cast the spell a limited number of times per day.
Magic Items.
Spell Scrolls and some other magic items contain spells that can be cast without a
. The description of such an item specifies how many times a spell can be cast from it.
When a spellcaster casts a spell using a slot that is of a higher level than the spell, the spell takes on the higher level for that casting. For instance, if a Wizard casts
using a level 2 slot, that
Magic Missile
is level 2. Effectively, the spell expands to fill the slot it is put into.
Some spells, such as
and
, have more powerful effects when cast at a higher level, as detailed in a spell's description.

docs.title: Spell Range Rules
docs.excerpt: Explains how a spell's range (Distance, Touch, Self) determines where its effect can originate.

text.text: A spell's range indicates how far from the spellcaster the spell's effect can originate, and the spell's description specifies which part of the effect is limited by the range.
A range usually takes one of the following forms:
Distance.
The range is expressed in feet.
Touch.
The spell's effect originates on something, as defined by the spell, that the spellcaster must touch within their reach.
Self.
The spell is cast on the spellcaster or emanates from them, as specified in the spell.
If a spell has movable effects, they aren't restricted by its range unless the spell's description says otherwise.

links copied from spell canonical snapshot blocks.
It is not runtime spell truth. It is a cross-link surface that shows which glossary/rules concepts are being cited by the canonical spell pages captured so far.

Bright Light
Description: Bright Light Bright Light lets most creatures see normally.
Referenced By 13 Spell(s): Conjure Celestial, Continual Flame, Darkness, Daylight, Fire Shield, Flame Blade, Flaming Sphere, Hallow, Light, Prismatic Wall, Produce Flame, Shining Smite, Sunbeam
Concentration
Description: Concentration Some spells require you to maintain concentration in order to keep their magic active.
Referenced By 10 Spell(s): Dominate Beast, Dominate Monster, Dominate Person, Earthquake, Flesh to Stone, Glyph of Warding, Hex, Major Image, Sleet Storm, Wall of Stone
Cone
Description: Cone A cone is a three dimensional spatial volume that projects from a point of origin and expands proportionally as it travels.
Referenced By 7 Spell(s): Burning Hands, Color Spray, Cone of Cold, Conjure Barrage, Dragon's Breath, Fear, Prismatic Spray
Cube
Description: Cube A cube is a three dimensional spatial volume defined by equal measurements of length, width, and height.

Referenced By 22 Spell(s): Cloud of Daggers, Control Water, Create or Destroy Water, Creation, Disintegrate, Fabricate, Faerie Fire, Fire Storm, Forcecage, Hallucinatory Terrain, Heroes' Feast, Hypnotic Pattern, Major Image, Minor Illusion, Mordenkainen's Magnificent Mansion, Mordenkainen's Private Sanctum, Phantasmal Force, Programmed Illusion, Silent Image, Slow, Thunderwave, Web
Curse
Description: Curse A curse is a harmful effect attached to a creature, object, place, or ongoing state.
Referenced By 3 Spell(s): Hex, Remove Curse, True Resurrection
Cylinder
Description: Cylinder A cylinder is a three dimensional spatial volume defined by a circular base and a vertical column of height.
Referenced By 8 Spell(s): Call Lightning, Conjure Celestial, Flame Strike, Ice Storm, Magic Circle, Moonbeam, Reverse Gravity, Sleet Storm

Referenced By 3 Spell(s): Foresight, Ray of Enfeeblement, Resurrection

Difficult Terrain
Description: Difficult Terrain Difficult terrain is ground, material, or space that slows movement because a creature cannot cross it as efficiently as open, stable ground.

Referenced By 17 Spell(s): Blade Barrier, Conjure Minor Elementals, Earthquake, Entangle, Evard's Black Tentacles, Freedom of Movement, Grease, Guardian of Nature, Hunger of Hadar, Ice Storm, Insect Plague, Mirage Arcane, Sleet Storm, Speak with Plants, Spike Growth, Storm of Vengeance, Web
Dim Light
Description: Dim Light Dim Light, also called shadows, creates a Lightly Obscured area .

Referenced By 15 Spell(s): Continual Flame, Darkness, Daylight, Faerie Fire, Fire Shield, Flame Blade, Flaming Sphere, Leomund's Tiny Hut, Light, Moonbeam, Prismatic Wall, Produce Flame, Starry Wisp, Sunbeam, Symbol
Emanation
Description: Emanation An emanation is a three dimensional area of effect that projects outward in all directions from a creature or object.

Description: Friendly A friendly attitude describes a creature that presently regards another creature with goodwill.
Referenced By 4 Spell(s): Charm Monster, Charm Person, Simulacrum, True Polymorph
Half Cover
Description: Half Cover Half cover represents partial physical screening by an intervening object, creature, or obstacle.
Referenced By 1 Spell(s): Sacred Flame
Heavily Obscured
Description: Heavily Obscured A heavily obscured area blocks ordinary sight so completely that creatures cannot see clearly into it, through it, or within it using normal vision.
Referenced By 9 Spell(s): Cloudkill, Druid Grove, Fog Cloud, Guards and Wards, Incendiary Cloud, Shadow of Moil, Sleet Storm, Stinking Cloud, Storm of Vengeance
Hostile
Description: Hostile A hostile attitude describes a creature that currently opposes another creature and is disposed against that creature's interests.
Referenced By 2 Spell(s): Calm Emotions, Glyph of Warding
Indifferent
Description: Indifferent An indifferent attitude describes a creature that currently has no strong favorable or hostile investment in another creature's outcome.
Referenced By 1 Spell(s): Calm Emotions
Lightly Obscured
Description: Lightly Obscured A lightly obscured area interferes with sight enough to reduce clarity without fully shutting vision down.
Referenced By 2 Spell(s): Insect Plague, Web
Line
Description: Line A line is a three dimensional area of effect that extends in a straight path from a point of origin.
Referenced By 3 Spell(s): Gust of Wind, Lightning Bolt, Sunbeam
Passive Perception
Description: Passive Perception Passive Perception is a creature's ongoing background awareness when it is not spending time or an action on deliberate searching.

Description: Possessed A creature is possessed when another will, presence, or force is currently occupying or directing it.

Description: Shape Shifting Shape shifting is a transformation state that changes a creature's body into another form or materially rewrites that body's outward structure.

Description: Spell Duration Rules A spell's duration is the length of time the spell persists after it is cast.

Description: Spell Effects (Targets, Saves, Attacks) The effects of a spell are detailed after its duration entry.

Description: Spell Range Rules A spell's range indicates how far from the spellcaster the spell's effect can originate, and the spell's description specifies which part of the effect is limited by the range.

Sphere

Description: A Sphere is an area of effect that extends in straight lines from a point of origin outward in all directions. The effect that creates a Sphere specifies the distance it extends as the radius of the Sphere. A Sphere's point of origin is included in the Sphere's area of effect.

Referenced By 28 Spell(s): Acid Splash, Calm Emotions, Circle of Death, Cloudkill, Confusion, Darkness, Daylight, Delayed Blast Fireball, Fireball, Fog Cloud, Glyph of Warding, Hunger of Hadar, Incendiary Cloud, Insect Plague, Meteor Swarm, Otiluke's Freezing Sphere, Plant Growth, Purify Food and Drink, Shatter, Silence, Sleep, Spike Growth, Stinking Cloud, Sunburst, Symbol, Vitriolic Sphere, Weird, Zone of Truth

Description: Stable A stable creature at 0 hit points is no longer in the immediate death save crisis caused by that collapse.
Referenced By 1 Spell(s): Spare the Dying

Description: Temporary Hit Points Some spells and special abilities confer temporary hit points to a creature.
Referenced By 8 Spell(s): Animal Shapes, Armor of Agathys, False Life, Guardian of Nature, Heroism, Polymorph, Shapechange, True Polymorph
Three-Quarters Cover
Description: Three Quarters Cover Three quarters cover represents significant physical screening by an intervening obstacle.
Referenced By 2 Spell(s): Blade Barrier, Sacred Flame
Total Cover
Description: Total Cover Total cover represents a complete physical obstruction or opaque barrier standing between the source of an effect or attack and a target.
Referenced By 3 Spell(s): Draconic Transformation, Enervation, Witch Bolt

docs.title: Gemini
docs.excerpt: The advanced AI model powering this world's narrative and interactions.

text.text: The advanced AI model powering Aralia's narrative and interactions. Gemini acts as the Dungeon Master, the storyteller, and the architect of the world, weaving together the threads of player choices and procedural generation to create a unique and immersive experience.
